kalender.e: From Middle Dutch kalendier, from Old French calendier, from Latin calendārium. The form ending on -er was influenced by German Kalender.

a similarly closed social class, especially if rather impenetrable -notably (de facto) hereditary-, as in Ancient Egypt or various tribal and feudal societies
any closed, social and/or hierarchic group, such as a priesthood or a military corps

kaste.e: From Portuguese casta (“lineage, race”), from the feminine of casto (“pure, chaste”), from Latin castus (“pure, clean”). Cognate with Danish, Norwegian (see below), German Kaste, English caste, French caste etc.

kakapo.e: Borrowed from English kakapo, from Māori kākāpō.

examples: Nog een kopje thee?
Buldogs hebben een angstaanjagende kop.
Hou je kop!
Wat een kwaaie kop!

s.def: head of a nail, pin etc.
examples: Je slaat de spijker op de kop.

s.def: front, lead, e.g. in a race; charge, control
examples: De underdog ligt op kop.

s.def: pegbox (part of a stringed instrument that holds the tuning pegs)

kop.e: From Middle Dutch cop, probably from Late Latin cuppa.

s.def: cow; a female of certain mammals, in particular bovines

koe.e: From Middle Dutch coe, from Old Dutch cuo, from Proto-West Germanic *kō (“cow”), from Proto-Germanic *kōz (“cow”).

examples:
1789, Justus Christiaan Hennings (= Justus Christian Hennings), Onzydige en beproefde gedagten, over de leer aangaande geesten en geesten-zieners, vol. 3, tr. from German, Arend Fokke Simonszoon (publ.), page 324.
Ik kan my heel wél te binnen brengen, dat deze perzoonen, naderhand, veel geruster en veiliger hebben huisgehouden, toen het eens was beslist, dat Spooken en Kobolden by hen niet wierden aangenomen.
Het volk in de nabijheid der hooge bergmeren gelooft nog aan allerlei kobolden, elfen, nikkers, water- en berggeesten.

kobold.e: 18th century. Borrowed from German Kobold. Doublet with kabouter.

examples: Het kwik is vannacht tot onder nul gedaald.

koffie.e: Borrowed from Italian caffè, from Ottoman Turkish قهوه (kahve), from Arabic قَهْوَة (qahwa).

s.def: a cabbage, plant of genus Brassica
the edible leaves of a Brassica

kool.e: From Middle Dutch col, cole, from Old Dutch *kōl, *kōla, from Latin caulis.

s.def: Kosovo (a partly-recognized country on the Balkan Peninsula in Southeast Europe)

examples: Ik lees elke ochtend de krant.
Kun je de krant doorgeven?

krant.e: From earlier courant, from French courant.

kaars.e: From Middle Dutch kerse, cāerse, from Old High German kerza, from either Latin cērāta or Latin charta. See German Kerze for more.

examples: Hij klopt op de deur.
Hij is met 5-0 vernederend geklopt.

s.def: to be correct, to be true
examples: Klopt het dat jij niet van pannenkoeken houdt?

s.def: to fit, work out, like a calculation; to make sense, to be true
examples: Ik had Stephen gevraagd om een vertaling, maar ik weet niet of het echt klopt.

s.def: to lead the devoutly professed, unmarried but non-congregational life of a Catholic klop

examples: Dan net iets verderop, achter een schutting, waarvoor mensen op hun bus op hun benen staan te wachten. Een man, tussen dat groen van boesboesi. Staat zijn tollie te kloppen, want hij heeft een ekstra lust na slaapgang.

kloppen.e: From Middle Dutch cloppen, from Old Dutch *cloppōn, probably onomatopoeic. Cognate with German klopfen and kloppen.

examples: Welke zot heb me schoorsteen verstopt met kado's? (Hollandic)

s.def: any large, opulent building, in particular a residence
a raised fortification on late mediaeval ships

kasteel.e: From Middle Dutch castēel, from Old Northern French castel, from Latin castellum. Doublet of Kessel, chateau, Castilië, castellum, ketella, and telo.

kantoor.e: From Middle Dutch cantoor, from Old French comtoir.

examples: de Franse keuken

keuken.e: From Middle Dutch cuekene, from Old Dutch *cukina, from Proto-West Germanic *kukinā, from Late Latin cocīna, from earlier coquīna.

s.def: a surname originating as an occupation, meaning cook

kok.e: From Middle Dutch coc, from Latin coquus.

koning.e: From Middle Dutch cōninc, from Old Dutch cuninc, from Proto-West Germanic *kuning, from Proto-Germanic *kuningaz. Cognate to English king.

koningin.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch coninginne, inherited from Old Dutch kuninginna. By surface analysis, koning (“king”) + -in (nominal feminine suffix).

koorts.e:
From Middle Dutch coorts, corts, curts. Related to Middle Low German korts (“fever”), possibly abbreviated from a phrase containing korts (“short”), from Middle High German kurz.
An indirect relationship with kool (“coal”) has been suggested, via Proto-Indo-European *ǵwelH- (“burn, shine”); compa...

s.def: a kneeing, a prod or blow with the knee

knie.e: From Middle Dutch cnie, from Old Dutch *cnio, from Proto-West Germanic *kneu, from Proto-Germanic *knewą, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵónu.

kans.e: From Middle Dutch canse, borrowed from Old Northern French caance (compare central cheance), from Vulgar Latin *cadentia, from cadere (“to fall”), referring to the falling of dice. Doublet of cadans, cadens, and sjans. Cognate with French chance, English chance.

examples: Ik zal de blauwe trui kiezen voor het feest.
Je moet een van de opties kiezen die het beste bij je past.
Burgers hebben het recht om hun stem uit te kiezen tijdens verkiezingen.
Kom op tijd naar het stembureau om je stem uit te kiezen.
De leden zullen binnenkort een nieuwe voorzitter kiezen voor de vereniging.
Het volk zal de nieuwe president van het land kiezen in de komende verkiezingen.

kiezen.e: From Middle Dutch kiesen, from Old Dutch *kiesan, from Proto-West Germanic *keusan, from Proto-Germanic *keusaną, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵews-.

examples: Ik ga morgen een nieuwe telefoon kopen.
We hebben gisteren een nieuwe auto gekocht.

kopen.e: From Middle Dutch côpen, from Old Dutch cōpon, from Proto-West Germanic *kaupōn, from Proto-Germanic *kaupōną, from Latin caupō.

kennis.e: From Middle Dutch kennesse. Equivalent to kennen + -nis.

koken.e: From Middle Dutch cōken, from Old Dutch *kokon, from Proto-West Germanic *kokōn.

examples: Het kostte me wat tijd om het af te maken.

s.def: to get, to receive, to come into possession of (anything ranging from concrete to abstract inputs such as news, gift, punishment, et cetera)
examples: Ze heeft van haar familie een huwelijkskado gekregen.
Ik weet niet waar hij dat idee heeft gekregen, maar er klopt niets van.

s.def: to get, to be presented with
examples: Ik heb geprobeerd mijn televisie aan te sluiten, maar ik krijg alleen sneeuw.

s.def: to get, to (manage to) cause to become
examples: Ik krijg mijn auto niet gestart!

s.def: Used to form a passive sentence with a ditransitive verb, with the original indirect object becoming the subject of krijgen.
examples: Hij kreeg een prijs uitgereikt.

s.def: to catch (a disease), to become ill with
examples: Ik heb een verkoudheid gekregen.
Krijg de tering!

Judges 1, 3, Statenvertaling.
Toen zeide Juda tot zijn broeder Simeon: Trek met mij op in mijn lot, en laat ons tegen de Kanaänieten krijgen, zo zal ik ook met u optrekken in uw lot.

krijgen.e: From Middle Dutch crigen, from Old Dutch *crīgan, from Proto-West Germanic *krīgan. Cognate to German kriegen (“to get”).

s.def: to know (a person, a thing), be acquainted with, to have knowledge of the properties of a particular person, object or concept, through personal experience, teaching, practice, or habit; to be familiar with
examples: Lars kent Emma, weet je dat? — Ja, ik weet dat Lars Emma kent.
Ik ken Rusland niet, ik weet niets over dat land.
Zijn werk kende vroeger veel kritiek, maar wordt tegenwoordig geaccepteerd.

kennen.e: From Middle Dutch kennen, from Old Dutch kennen, from Proto-West Germanic *kannijan, from Proto-Germanic *kannijaną, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵneh₃-.

examples: De airco is kapot en nu zweet ik als een otter.
Ik ben altijd helemaal kapot na zo'n lange dag.
Ik moest kapot hard fietsen om op tijd te komen.

kapot.e: From earlier capot, from French capot (“defeated, destroyed”).

kaart.e: From Middle Dutch cāerte, from Old French carte, from Latin charta.

kast.e: From Middle Dutch caste (“chest”), from Old Dutch *casto, from Proto-West Germanic *kastō (“chest, reservoir”).

s.def: a boot or large shoe, especially an army boot

kist.e: From Middle Dutch kiste, from Old Dutch *kista, from Proto-West Germanic *kistu, from Latin cista, from Ancient Greek κίστη (kístē), from Proto-Indo-European *kisteh₂.

s.def: travel class (such as first class or economy class)

klas.e: Borrowed from French classe, from Latin classis.

examples: Die kerel is echt klasse.
Haar prestaties op het wereldkampioenschap waren klasse.

klasse.e: Borrowed from French classe, from Latin classis.

examples: Hij droeg een houten kruis om zijn nek.
Teken een kruis in het vakje.

s.def: cross (such shape as used in heraldry)
examples: Het familiewapen had een gouden kruis op een blauw veld.
Jezus werd gekruisigd op een kruis.
Iedereen heeft zijn eigen kruis te dragen.
De broek scheurde bij zijn kruis.
De noot had een kruis, wat betekent dat hij een halve toon hoger moet worden gespeeld.
Kies je kruis of munt?

kruis.e: From Middle Dutch crûce, from Old Dutch *krūci, from Latin crux (“cross”).

kuil.e: From Middle Dutch cule, from Old Dutch *kūla, from Proto-Germanic *kūlō, related to *kūlǭ (“hole”).

s.def: to cut (remove an item and place it in memory for later use)
to make a pinching movement, especially with one's eyelids; thus, to blink

knippen.e:
From Middle Dutch knippen, further etymology unknown. Directly related only with Middle Low German knippen. Probably onomatopoeic.
The sense “to blink” may, at least in part, be due to conflation with knijpen (“to squeeze”). Compare de ogen toeknijpen (“to screw up one’s eyes”); also Low German k...

kraan.e: From Middle Dutch crāne, from Old Dutch *crano, from Proto-West Germanic *kranō, from Proto-Germanic *kranô. The senses “tap” and “crane” as a machine are both based on resemblance with the bird's neck.

examples: De koffie is zó klaar.

s.def: finished, done (of a person performing a task)
examples: Zij is klaar met haar werk.

1968, Willem Johan van der Molen & Jan Wit, "Evenals een moede hinde" (psalm 42).
Evenals een moede hinde / naar het klare water smacht, / schreeuwt mijn ziel om God te vinden / die ik ademloos verwacht.

klaar.e: From Middle Dutch clâer (“bright, shining, ready, finished”), from Latin clārus (“bright”).

s.def: a clump, nugget, lump (an unshaped piece or mass)
a kicker, protective footwear worn by goalkeepers

klomp.e: From Middle Dutch clompe (“lump or mass of metal, wooden shoe, clump”), from Old Dutch *klumpo, from Proto-Germanic *klumpô (“lump, clump, mass; clasp”), from Proto-Indo-European *glembʰ- (“clamp, mass”). Cognate with Old Frisian klumpa, English clump, Low German Klump, German Klumpen. Related to...

examples: We gaan gewoon naar de kroeg.

kroeg.e: Possibly borrowed from Middle Low German krôch or its dialectal variant krûch. Cognate with German Krug.

s.def: Croatia (a country on the Balkan Peninsula in Southeast Europe)
clove, the dried, often ground flower buds of Syzygium aromaticum

kus.e:
From Middle Dutch kos, kus, from Old Dutch *kos, *kus, from Proto-West Germanic *koss, from Proto-Germanic *kussaz.
The older Dutch forms with -u- are taken from the verb, those with -o- derive directly from the noun. Compare German Kuss, English kiss, Danish kys.

s.def: to fit someone, to suit someone (of a piece of clothing)

kleden.e: From Middle Dutch clêden. Equivalent to kleed + -en.

s.def: a traditional Surinamese dress, traditionally worn by women of African descent
a koto; a Japanese stringed instrument

koto.e: Borrowed from Sranan Tongo koto, from English coat.

komkommer.e: From Middle Dutch cucumer, from Old French concombre, from Latin cucumis.

khoisan.e: Borrowed from English Khoisan. Equivalent to Khoi + San, names of two unrelated peoples in Southern Africa.

kunne.e: From Middle Dutch kunne, from Old Dutch *kunni, from Proto-West Germanic *kuni, from Proto-Germanic *kunją, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵenh₁- (“to produce”).

s.def: a hamlet in Gemert-Bakel, North Brabant, Netherlands

koks.e: First attested as Koks in 1794. Derived from Cox (a surname). Named after a family that was resident near the current site of the hamlet.

s.def: Cape Verde (an archipelago and country in West Africa)
Kuwait (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)
Kazakhstan (a country in Central Asia and Eastern Europe)
Kyrgyzstan (a country in Central Asia, bordering on Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan and China)
initialism of Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, a Flemish university in Belgium
marble (small ball used in various games)

kul.e: From Middle Dutch cul, kul (“testicle”), from Old French coille, from Latin cōleus (“testicle”).

kever.e: From Middle Dutch kever, from Old Dutch kevero, from Proto-West Germanic *kefrō. Akin to English chafer, German Käfer.

kap.e: From Middle Dutch cappe, from Late Latin cappa.

s.def: a trawl, a dragnet used for trawling over or close to the seabed

kor.e: From earlier korre, assimilated from earlier korde with metathesis r < krode, from Middle Dutch crode (“wheelbarrow”), from the verb cruden (“to push forward, slide”).

s.def: a cart, vehicle on wheels without motor
any wheeled vehicle, in particular a car or truck

kar.e: From Middle Dutch carre, from Latin carrus or the mediaeval variant carra, from Gaulish carros. Doublet of ros.

s.def: barrier, retaining wall (structure designed to keep water or earth in or out)
staple (U-shaped metal fastener to bind materials together, for example to attach fence wire to posts)

kram.e: From Middle Dutch cramme, from Middle Dutch crimmen (“to catch, grab (with claws)”), from Proto-West Germanic *krimman.

examples: (Belgium)

s.def: a public or private entity, mainly concerned with managing funds for a certain purpose, after which it is often named
a counter, room etc. where cash payments are made and/or received

examples: geld in kas

kas.e: From Middle Dutch casse, from Old French casse, from Italian cassa, from Latin capsa. Doublet of cash.

s.def: choir, part of a church building

koor.e: From Middle Dutch côor, from Latin chorus.

s.def: kill, waterway on sand flats or mud flats

kil.e:
From Middle Dutch kille, from Proto-Germanic *kaliz, related to Middle Dutch killen (“to be (ice) cold”).
Related also to German Low German Köle, German kühl, Danish køle, Swedish kyla, Icelandic kylur, and English chill.

examples: Het schip kapseisde

s.def: a cunt (an extremely unpleasant or objectionable person)

kont.e: From Middle Dutch conte (“vagina”, also generally “sex organ”), from Old Dutch *kunta (“vagina”), from Proto-Germanic *kuntǭ. Compare English cunt.

s.def: to slate, to criticise heavily, in a snide manner
to make/ give (a) side(s), edge etc.
to let graze along the side of a pasture
to tilt, notably on a side
to touch at a side; to oppose in contact

kanten.e: From kant (“lace”) + -en, possibly of Celtic origin.

s.def: chandelier with more than two arms

kroon.e: From Middle Dutch crône, from Old Dutch corōna, from Proto-West Germanic *korōnā, from Latin corōna. Doublet of kruin and corona.

s.def: a coin of 25 cents (0.25 guilder), quarter
quarter of a pill of recreational drugs, particularly ecstasy

korf.e: From Middle Dutch corf, from Old Dutch *korf, from Proto-West Germanic *korb.

s.def: Kinshasa (the capital city of the Democratic Republic of the Congo)
stool (piece of furniture without back or armrests)

kruk.e: From Middle Dutch crucke, from Old Dutch *krukka, from Frankish *krukkju, from Proto-Germanic *krukjō.

s.def: a drain that is part a sewer system
a small waterway connected to one or several windmills that control the water level in a polder, serving as a conduit to remove surplus water
a dyke beside such a waterway
a stagnant pool or lake resulting from the vortex of e.g. a breached dyke
the body of water between sluice gates

kolk.e: From Middle Dutch colc, from Old Dutch *kolk, from Proto-West Germanic *kolk (“hollow, pit, gully”), from Proto-Germanic *kulukaz, an extension of *kelǭ (“throat”).

s.def: vulva, especially the vagina; cunt, pussy
cunt, a strongly disliked person, usually towards women
examples: Verrek, diene stomme kut hèt mèn wer gevat. (a North Brabantian dialect)
Wa bende gij toch een domme kut.
Nou, dat was kut.

kut.e: Derivation from Proto-Germanic *kweþuz (“abdomen, belly”) — compare Old Norse kviðr (“abdomen, belly”) and Gothic 𐌵𐌹𐌸𐌿𐍃 (qiþus, “womb”) — is unlikely. Probably kut is cognate with kuit (“spawn”) and kont (“ass”). Also Old Dutch quintuc (“genitals of a female dog”) [8th century] might be related.

examples: Een spin in de morgen brengt kommer en zorgen.

kommer.e:
From Middle Dutch commer, comber. Multiple etymologies have been proposed:
* Often said to be from Old French combre (“hindrance, barrier”), combrer (“to hinder”), from Medieval Latin combrus (“barricade”), usually said to be from either Latin cumulus (“heap”) or Gaulish *komberū << Proto-Celtic ...

krank.e: From Middle Dutch cranc, from Old Dutch *cranc, from Proto-West Germanic *krank. Related to krenken (“to hurt”).

s.def: mantle (incandescent covering of a heat source)

kous.e: From Middle Dutch couse, from Picard Old French cauce, from Vulgar Latin calcea, from Latin calceus.

kozak.e: From Middle French cosaque, from Russian каза́к (kazák) and Ukrainian коза́к (kozák), from a Turkic word meaning “free man, wanderer”. Cognate with Kazakh.

s.def: the white flesh of the coconut, often in a desiccated form

kokos.e: Borrowed from Spanish coco or Portuguese coco. The final s was originally a plural ending.

kunde.e: From Middle Dutch conde, cunde, from Old Dutch *kundi, from Proto-Germanic *kundīn (“being known”), derivation with grammatical change from *kunþaz (“known”).

s.def: someone who's an expert at something, whiz
examples: Ze is een kei in wiskunde.

kei.e: From Middle Dutch key, keye, from Old Dutch *kei (in toponym keidīk (“Keidijk”)), perhaps from Proto-West Germanic *kagi (compare kegel (“cone”)), from Proto-Germanic *kagiz.

komma.e: Borrowed from French comma, from Latin comma, from Ancient Greek κόμμα (kómma).

s.def: kettle, cauldron (a pot used to boil water)

ketel.e: From Middle Dutch ketel, from Old Dutch *ketil, from Proto-Germanic *katilaz, from Late Latin catillus.

s.def: an extremely poor person, a poor sod

kramp.e: From Middle Dutch crampe, from Old Dutch *crampo, from Frankish *krampa, from Proto-Germanic *krampô, related to *krampō.

s.def: A cod, fish of the family Gadidae.

kabeljauw.e: From Middle Dutch cabelliau, from Old Dutch *cabillao, possibly from Latin baculum (“stick, staff”), referring to the way cod were split and dried on wooden sticks. Or, with metathesis from/influenced by Basque bakailao (“cod”), Portuguese bacalhau (“cod”).

kaai.e: From Middle Dutch caey, possibly via Old French cai from Proto-Celtic *kayos.

s.def: to jam, to not move smoothly

klemmen.e: From Middle Dutch clemmen, from Old Dutch *klemmen, from Proto-West Germanic *klammjan.

examples: Zij houdt samen met haar man kanaries in een kooi.
De visser lag al in zijn kooi.

kooi.e: From Middle Dutch coye, ultimately from Latin cavea. Compare German Koje.

kurk.e: From Middle Dutch curc, from Spanish corcho, ultimately from Latin cortex.

s.def: a hamlet in Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

korn.e: First attested as den Korne in 1542. Derived from the hydronym Korn.

kwakzalver.e: From kwakzalven + -er, from kwak (“quack”) + zalven (“to anoint”). Also compare quacken, kwaken (“to prattle”).

kracht.e: From Middle Dutch cracht, from Old Dutch craft, from Proto-West Germanic *kraftu, from Proto-Germanic *kraftuz.

s.def: chapel, small Christian place of worship
shrine, small non-Christian temple or sanctuary
a hamlet in Lingewaard, Gelderland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Heiloo, North Holland, Netherlands

kapel.e: From Middle Dutch capelle, ultimately from Latin cappella.

s.def: church, temple, non-Christian house of worship

kerk.e: From Middle Dutch kerke, from Old Dutch kirica, from Proto-West Germanic *kirikā, an early borrowing from Ancient Greek κυριακόν (kuriakón).

kathedraal.e: From Middle Dutch cathedrael, from Middle French cathedral, from Latin cathedrālis. Originally an adjective, used as a noun as early as late Middle Dutch.

s.def: a hamlet in Coevorden, Drenthe, Netherlands
a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

klooster.e: From Middle Dutch clôoster, from Old Dutch *clōster, post-West Germanic borrowing from Medieval Latin claustrum.

s.def: cardinal (high-ranking official in the Catholic Church, Prince of the Church)

kardinaal.e: From Middle Dutch cardinael, from Latin cardinālis (episcopus).

kapitaal.e: From Italian capitale, from Latin capitālis (“of the head”) (in sense “head of cattle”), from caput (“head”).

s.def: cancer, something harmful that festers and spreads elsewhere
An expression of anger, annoyance, etc. damn, fuck

kanker.e: From Middle Dutch canker, from Latin cancer. Doublet of sjanker.

ket.e: From Middle Dutch *kitte, from Old Dutch kitto (“fawn, kid”), from Proto-West Germanic *kittō (“fawn, chit”). Compare English chit. Compare also West Frisian kedde (“pony”), English, Swedish and Danish kid, German Kitz and Kitze, Albanian kedh and kec.

kletsnat.e:
Compound of kletsen + nat.
Compare German klatschnass.

s.def: a weight defined as one-hundredth of a pikol, about 625 (other source: 616.7) grams, used in China, Indonesia and Japan
examples: Aardappelen zijn er haast niet en een tijdje geleden vroegen ze er 27 cent per kati voor !!!

s.def: a leaden box fitting such weight, notably to ship tea

kati.e: Borrowed from Malay kati, see below. Cognate of English catty.

krater.e: Borrowed from Latin crātēr, from Ancient Greek κρᾱτήρ (krātḗr).

examples: Ik heb nieuwe kleren gekocht.
Haar kleren waren nat van de regen.

kleren.e: Alternative form of klederen (with regular loss of intervocalic -d-; compare leder / leer), archaic plural form of kleed.

s.def: Crimea (a geographic region and peninsula in Eastern Europe, jutting out into the Black Sea; de facto occupied and annexed in 2014 as a republic of Russia, but internationally recognized as an autonomous republic of Ukraine :)

krim.e: Ultimately from Crimean Tatar Qırım, probably via German or a Slavic language; the gender suggests either that the direct source is German or that the word was influenced by a Romance language.

s.def: a hamlet in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

kade.e:
From Middle Dutch cade, later also caey, from a Celtic word for "field, hedge," from Proto-Celtic *kagyom (“enclosure”). The word may have been borrowed through Old French cai.
A hypercorrection of kaai, as the original word was thought to have contained -d-, which tends to be replaced by -j- in ...

kaka.e: Borrowed from English kaka, from Māori kākā.

s.def: a hamlet in Dijk en Waard, North Holland, Netherlands

kabel.e: Attested as Kabel (De) in 1899. Derived from a dialectal form of kavel (“lot, parcel”).

s.def: bald, bare, without fur, plumage, foliage etc.
bald, bare, without fur, plumage, foliage etc.
skinned, without means, in a poor state

kaal.e: From Middle Dutch cale, from Old Dutch *kalo, from Proto-West Germanic *kalu, from Proto-Germanic *kalwaz (“naked, bald”), from Proto-Indo-European *gelH- (“naked, bald”). Compare German kahl, West Frisian keal, English callow.

s.def: a hamlet in Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

kille.e: Borrowed from Yiddish קהילה (kehile, “(Jewish) community”), from Hebrew קְהִלָּה (k'hillá, “congregation”).

examples: Het veld stond vol met rijpe aren vol met koren.
Koren is een belangrijke voedselbron voor mens en dier.

s.def: to be sick, to have a tendency to vomit; to vomit

koren.e:
From Middle Dutch koren, corn, from Old Dutch korn, coren, from Proto-West Germanic *korn, from Proto-Germanic *kurną, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵr̥h₂nóm.
Compare Low German Koorn, German Korn, English corn, Danish korn. Doublet of graan.

kou.e: From older koude, from Middle Dutch coude, alteration (based on the adjective) of kelde, from Old Dutch *keldi, from Proto-West Germanic *kaldī. Cognate with German Kälte and English cold (noun).

examples: Lief klein konijntje had een vliegje op zijn neus.

s.def: Kandahar (a city, the capital of the province of Kandahar in southern Afghanistan)
kart, cart, go-kart, go-cart (small vehicle used for racing)
to give out a dry and brief cough

kuchen.e: From Middle Dutch cuchen, coechen, cochen, ultimately from Proto-Germanic *kuh- (“to cough”), likely of onomatopoeic origin. Akin to English cough, German keuchen.

kraam.e: From Middle Dutch craem, from Middle High German krām, from Old High German krām. Probably from a Slavic language, such as Old Church Slavonic грамъ (gramŭ, “pub, inn”) or чрѣмъ (črěmŭ, “tent”).

s.def: a cherry tree, one of certain trees of the genus Prunus, especially the subgenus Cerasus
a cherry, a stone fruit of a cherry tree
cress: a term used of various plants, chiefly but not solely of the family Brassicaceae (crucifers), particularly of the genus Rorippa

kers.e: From Middle Dutch kerse, ultimately from Vulgar Latin ceresia, from the neuter plural of Late Latin ceresium, from Latin cerasium, from Ancient Greek κεράσιον (kerásion, “cherry”), from κερασός (kerasós, “bird cherry”), ultimately possibly of Anatolian origin.

examples: De eekhoorn knabbelde op een nootje.

knabbelen.e: Compare German knabbern (“to nibble”). Both could ultimately be from Proto-Indo-European *gnet- (“to press”), or imitative.

kerstmis.e: From Middle Dutch Kersmisse. Equivalent to Kerst (“Christ”) + mis (“mass”).

keizerpinguïn.e: Compound of keizer + pinguïn, possibly a calque.

examples: Koninklijke Nederlandse Shell

koninklijk.e: From Middle Dutch coninclijc, from Old Dutch cuninclīc. Equivalent to koning + -lijk.

s.def: a course of a plane or ship
the price of a stock or bond, rate, notably on an exchange

koers.e: From Middle Dutch coers, from Middle French cours.

koop.e: From Middle Dutch côop, from Old Dutch *cōp, from Proto-Germanic *kaupaz. See the verb kopen (“to buy, trade”).

s.def: an unfashionable, large, bulky cell phone
examples: Floris stapte af, zette zijn fiets tegen een lantaarnpaal en slenterde langs een sprei met verroest gereedschap, een koffiezetapparaat zonder snoer, een mobiele telefoon model koelkast, een stapel gescheurde Donald Ducks, grammofoonplaten van Elvis Presley.

kletsen.e: Onomatopoeic, akin to klotsen and klutsen. Compare English clash and German klatschen.

kwetsen.e: From Middle Dutch quetsen, from Old Dutch quezzon, of uncertain origin but compare German quetschen. Early influence or even borrowing of the Old Dutch term from Old French quasser has been proposed.

kennelijk.e: From kennen (“to know, be acquainted with”) + -lijk.

s.def: crab, crustacean of the infraorder Brachyura

krab.e: From Middle Dutch crabbe, from Old Dutch *krabba.

examples: Maar verlos ons van het kwaad
Het kwaad is geschied.

kwaad.e: From Middle Dutch quâet, from Old Dutch *kwāt, from Proto-West Germanic *kwād.

kruipen.e: From Middle Dutch crupen, from Old Dutch *crūpan, from Proto-West Germanic *kreupan, from Proto-Germanic *kreupaną.

kandidaat.e: Borrowing from French candidat, from Latin candidātus.

s.def: to go well relationwise, to have a click
to squeal on someone, to snitch
to fall into place mentally (as in the penny drops)
to knock, to make a knocking sound

klikken.e: From Middle Dutch clicken, of imitative origin.

s.def: a press of a mouse button

korting.e: From Middle Dutch cortinge. Equivalent to korten + -ing.

examples: De middenklasse wordt verder gekort.

korten.e: From Middle Dutch corten. Equivalent to kort + -en.

examples: Als ik door de stad loop, vraag ik me vaak af: waarom zijn alle mensen, zo nors en zo kortaf? — As I walk through the city, I often wonder to myself: why are all the people, so rude and so abrupt? (KvK – Wakker met een wijsje)
De klant is altijd koning in deze winkel.
Hij is een aardige klant die vaak hier komt.

klant.e: From Middle Dutch calant (“person, friend”), from Old Northern French calant (compare regular Old French chalant), from Old French chaloir (“to heat, to care about”), from Latin caleō (“to be hot”).

s.def: a network of civil society organisations, healthcare institutions, government agencies, etc., working together to provide coordinated social or medical care to a specific category of patients or other citizens
to fool around noisily and hyperactively

keten.e: From Middle Dutch ketene, from Old Dutch *ketina, from Latin catēna. Compare Old High German ketina (German Kette).

klagen.e: From Middle Dutch clagen, from Old Dutch clagon, from Proto-West Germanic *klagōn (“to lament, complain”).

krachtig.e: From Middle Dutch crachtich, from Old Dutch krahtig. Equivalent to kracht + -ig.

s.def: any type of cake or biscuit

koek.e: From Middle Dutch coeke, from Old Dutch *kuoko, from Proto-Germanic *kōkô. Doublet of quiche.

s.def: bite-the-cake; a game, especially for children, in which piece(s) of cake are hung from a rope, and the children, being blindfolded, attempt to bite the piece(s) of cake

examples: "Juffra, 'k moe dringend naa de koer, of 't is in m'n broek!"

s.def: a political or social fraction or group

kamp.e: Borrowed from Middle French camp, from Latin campus (“open space; field”).

s.def: to turn around, to turn around an axis

keren.e: From Middle Dutch keren, from Old Dutch kēren, from Proto-Germanic *karzijaną, *kaurijaną.

s.def: to deal with, to suffer (from a problem), to cope
examples: Hij heeft met veel problemen te kampen. -- He has to deal with many problems.

s.def: Kampen (a municipality and seaside resort village on the island of Sylt, Schleswig-Holstein, Germany)
Kampen (a Hanseatic city and municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands)
Kampen (a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands)
Kampen (a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands)

kampen.e: From Middle Dutch campen, variant of kempen, ultimately from Latin campus (“battlefield”).

kankerlijer.e: Informal alternative form of kankerlijder (“person who suffers from cancer”).

s.def: a surname, Kuiper, originating as an occupation, derived from kuiper (“cooper”)

kuiper.e: From Middle Dutch cuper. Equivalent to kuipen + -er.

komst.e: From Middle Dutch comst, from Old Dutch cumst, from Proto-Germanic *kumþiz. Equivalent to komen (“to come”) + -st (“verbal noun”). Distant doublet of base and basis, via Proto-Indo-European *gʷḿ̥tis.

kantelen.e: From kanten (“to put on its sides”) + -elen.

s.def: critical (pertaining to a crisis or pivotal moment)
avocet, any bird of the genus Recurvirostra

kluut.e: Onomatopoeic, after its call. Forms with -ui- arose through regular sound change, but have been superseded by the older form with -uu-, which is truer to the bird's actual call.

s.def: A canary, a bird of the genus Serinus.

kanarie.e: Borrowed from French canari, from Spanish canario.

s.def: camel (Bactrian camel (Camelus bactrianus); animal of the genus Camelus)
examples: Een kameel kan lange afstanden afleggen zonder water.
De kameel draagt zware lasten door de woestijn.

kameel.e: From Middle Dutch camêel, from Latin camēlus, from Ancient Greek κάμηλος (kámēlos), from a Semitic language. The form kemel (with stress on the initial syllable), the earliest attested form of the word in Middle Dutch which survived into early Modern Dutch, presumably represents an earlier borrow...

s.def: a kangaroo, marsupial of the family Macropodidae

kangoeroe.e: Borrowed from English kangaroo; ultimately from Guugu Yimidhirr gangurru (“eastern grey kangaroo”).

s.def: a frog, relatively smooth amphibian of the order Anura
a cleat (device used for fastening), especially a nautical cleat

kikker.e: An onomatopoeia for the frog's sound. From kikken + -er. Compare West Frisian kikkert (“frog”).

s.def: rodent, any member of the order Rodentia

knaagdier.e: From knagen (“to gnaw”) + dier (“animal”), perhaps through German Nagetier, eventually after Latin rodens.

examples: Ga niet baden daar in de kali / en ga niet trouwen daar in Pesapen / Laat je raden, m’n kleine Ali / wanneer ik jou met raad van dienst wil zijn / (...) / Want een kaaiman zit in de kali / en ook een kaaiman zit in Pesapen / ’t Is je schoonma, m′n kleine Ali / die erop loert dat jij getrouwd zal zijn

krokodil.e: From Middle Dutch crocodil, from Latin crocodīlus, from Ancient Greek κροκόδειλος (krokódeilos).

s.def: a cuckoo, any bird of the family Cuculidae

examples: Die vent is helemaal koekoek.

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen, Limburg, Netherlands
a hamlet in Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

koekoek.e: From Middle Dutch coecoec, of imitative origin, perhaps influenced by Latin cuculus and/or Old French cucu (modern French coucou).

kleinkind.e: Compound of klein (“small, junior”) + kind (“child, descendant”), formed after kleinzoon and kleindochter.

konden.e: From Middle Dutch conden, from Old Dutch cunden, from Proto-West Germanic *kunþijan, from Proto-Germanic *kunþijaną. By surface analysis, kond + -en.

s.def: café au lait (coffee made with a large proportion of warm milk)

koffie verkeerd.e: From koffie (“coffee”) + verkeerd (“wrong”), thus “coffee the wrong way round”, meaning a lot of milk with coffee rather than the “normal” coffee with a bit of milk.

examples: In een koffiehoek van het KNMI turen we naar metingen afkomstig van de klimaatsatelliet Ceres, een kunstmaan die nauwgezet bijhoudt hoeveel zonnestraling er boven in de dampkring binnenkomt en hoeveel warmtestraling de aarde weer uitzendt, de ruimte in.

kunstmaan.e: From kunst- (“artificial”) + maan (“moon”). First used in 1958. Compare similarly formed words Hungarian műhold (1958) and Icelandic gervitungl (1955).

s.def: synthetic material; any material made artificially, through chemical means

kunststof.e: Borrowed from German Kunststoff. Equivalent to kunst- + stof.

s.def: one of certain related birds of the genus Corvus or of the family Corvidae
synonym of zwarte kraai (“carrion crow (Corvus corone)”)
a blue pennant indicating the presence of a navigation pilot

kraai.e: From Middle Dutch crâye, from Old Dutch *krāia, from Proto-West Germanic *krāā, from Proto-Germanic *krēǭ.

s.def: to crow, make to sound of roosters and certain other birds
to talk or cry affirmatively, in triumph, frolicking etc.

kraaien.e: From Middle Dutch crâyen, from Old Dutch crāien, from Proto-West Germanic *krāan (“to crow, shout”).

knuffelen.e: Unknown, but probably a sound-symbolic/expressive word. Compare Low German knuffeln, knüffeln, an iterative form of Low German knuffen (“to poke; bump; nudge”), West Frisian knoffelje (“to push; knock; bump”).

kieuw.e: From Middle Dutch kieuwe, from Old Dutch *kiowa, from Proto-West Germanic *keuwā.

s.def: a small boat used on inland waters
a neighbourhood of Sittard-Geleen, Limburg, Netherlands

kluis.e: From Middle Dutch cluse, from Old Dutch *klūsa, from Proto-West Germanic *klūsā.

kompas.e: From Middle Dutch compas (“circle, pair of compasses, compass”), from Old French compas.

klauw.e: From Middle Dutch clauwe, from Old Dutch *klawa, from Proto-Germanic *klawō. Compare German Klaue, English claw, Danish klo.

krans.e: From Middle Dutch crans, from Middle High German kranz, from Old High German kranz, probably altered from krenz, ultimately related to Proto-Germanic *kringaz (“circle, ring”).

s.def: a male given name, equivalent to English Charles

examples: Zijn voet zat klem tussen het rempedaal en de vloer.

klem.e: From Middle Dutch clemme. Equivalent to a deverbal from klemmen.

s.def: a high official's staff of close collaborators

examples: Onder groote belangstelling - ook de Gouverneur was aanwezig - werd Dinsdag en Donderdag de kinofilm vertoond, voorstellende den zegetocht van Hare Majesteit de Koningin door Limburg en Zeeuwsch Vlaaaderen^([sic – meaning Vlaanderen]) (…).

s.def: shrike, butcherbird, bird of the family Laniidae
clammy, damp, dank, moist and cool

klam.e: From Middle Dutch clam, ultimately from or related to Proto-West Germanic *klaimijan (“to smear with clay”).

s.def: relating to the quasi-federal structure of the Kingdom of the Netherlands; relating to matters pertaining all the constituent countries of that kingdom

koninkrijk.e: From Middle Dutch koninkrike, from Old Dutch *kuningrīki, from Proto-West Germanic *kuningarīkī, from Proto-Germanic *kuningarīkiją. Equivalent to koning + rijk. Compare German Königreich, Danish kongerige.

kapper.e: From kappen (“to cut or dress hair”) + -er.

s.def: Krakow (a city on the Vistula River, the capital of the Lesser Poland Voivodeship in southern Poland and the former capital (until 1596) of Poland as a whole)

krakau.e: From German Krakau, from Polish Kraków.

knijpen.e: From Middle Dutch knipen, from Old Dutch *knīpen, from Proto-West Germanic *knīpan, from Proto-Germanic *knīpaną. Likely related to nijpen.

kostbaar.e: From kost + -baar, calque of German kostbar, from Middle High German kostbære.

s.def: Klerk (“a surname originating as an occupation derived from klerk.”)

klerk.e: From Late Latin clēricus (“a priest, clergyman, cleric, also generally a learned man, clerk”), from Ancient Greek κληρικός (klērikós, “(adj. in church jargon) of the clergy”), from κλῆρος (klêros, “lot, inheritance,” originally “a shard used in casting lots”).

kokkel.e:
From Middle Dutch cockille (“shell”) and English cockle, both from French coquille (“shell”), from Latin conchula (“little shell”), the diminutive of Latin concha (“mussel; oyster”), from Ancient Greek κόγχη (kónkhē, “konkhē”).
The word is attested in modern Dutch in West-Flanders with indication...

kolos.e: Probably borrowed from Middle French colosse or otherwise directly from Latin colossus, from Ancient Greek κολοσσός (kolossós).

s.def: initialism of Koninklijke Luchtvaart Maatschappij (“Royal Dutch Airlines”, literally “Royal Aviation Company”)

koen.e: From Middle Dutch coene, from Old Dutch *cuoni, from Proto-West Germanic *kōnī, from Proto-Germanic *kōniz, the root of which also yielded the Dutch verb kunnen.

s.def: a coot; any bird of genus Fulica
synonym of meerkoet (“Eurasian coot, Fulica atra”)

koot.e: From Middle Dutch cote, from Proto-Germanic *kutō-, related to *kautōn- (“knuckle”), of unknown ultimate origin, with no certain cognates outside of Germanic. However, compare kuil (“hole in the ground”) and keutel (“hard animal dropping”), though Kroonen considers comparisons with the latter a s...

krimpen.e: From Middle Dutch crimpen, from Old Dutch *crimpan, from Proto-Germanic *krimpaną.

examples: De bel in de kerk begon te klinken toen de dienst begon.
De muziek uit de luidsprekers klinkt helder en zuiver.
We willen graag een toast uitbrengen, laten we de glazen klinken!
Na de ceremonie klinkten de gasten hun glazen om het gelukkige paar te feliciteren.

s.def: to nail, to rivet, to clinch

klinken.e: From Middle Dutch clingen, clinken, from Old Dutch *clingan, from Proto-Germanic *klinganą (“to sound”).

s.def: a child between the ages of four and seven years (before the onset of concrete operational thought)
a minor of any age, though chiefly up to 10 years
an adult or older child who behaves very immaturely
a hamlet in Maashorst, North Brabant, Netherlands

examples: De oude stad was vol met drukke kooplui die hun waren aanboden op de markt.
De koopman reisde naar verre landen om exotische goederen te verhandelen.

koopman.e: From Middle Dutch côopman, from Old Dutch kōpman, from Proto-West Germanic *kaupamann, equivalent to koop (“buy, trade”) + man.

s.def: a knob, roundish handle, ornament etc.
a button, control device to push etc.; metonymy: control, power to stop

knop.e:
Ultimately from Proto-Germanic *knappô, or a variant of it, one of many kn- words related to *knuttô (“a knot”).
Cognate with German Knopf and probably English knop. Also related with Dutch knob, knobbel, German Knubbel, which are cognate with English knob.

s.def: a row, unruliness, especially in keet schoppen; rumble, frolicking
a salt container; a salt boiling installation

keet.e: From Middle Dutch kete, of uncertain origin.

kopiëren.e: From Middle Dutch copiëren, possibly from French copier, ultimately from Latin copiō.

s.def: cricket, insect of the family Gryllidae

krekel.e: From Middle Dutch crekel, possibly a diminutive of an older form kreke (“cricket”). Ultimately of imitative origin and related to the root of kraken (“to crack”), with the suffix -el. Related to kriek, English creak.

s.def: a crust, a scab (hard, often flaky outer layer)

korst.e: From Middle Dutch corste, from Latin crusta.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

klei.e: From Middle Dutch cleie, from Old Dutch *klei, from Proto-West Germanic *klaij, from Proto-Germanic *klajjaz, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *gley- (“to glue, stick together”). Compare German Klei, English clay.

s.def: critical (expressing criticism, pertaining to criticism or critique)

kritisch.e: Borrowed from German kritisch, from French critique.

s.def: a size of type between insertio and petit, standardized as 7 point

kolonel.e: First attested 1548, from Middle French coronel, from Italian colonnello (“the officer of a small company of soldiers (column) that marched at the head of a regiment”), from compagna colonnella (“little column company”), from Latin columna (“pillar”), originally a collateral form of columen, cont...

examples: Kak, ik kom te laat.

kak.e: From Middle Dutch cac, deverbal from cacken; see kakken. Compare English cack.

examples: Deze peer heeft een kostelijke smaak.
Dat wordt weer een kostelijke maaltijd.
Ik heb me kostelijk vermaakt.

ketter.e: From Middle Dutch ketter, from Latin catharus (“Cathar”), from Ancient Greek καθαροί (katharoí, “the pure”), originally used by practitioners of Novatianism in relation to themselves.

s.def: Celt, person of Celtic origin, (historical) member of a Celtic tribe
examples: Het gebied tussen de Rijn en de Moezel was in deze tijd vooral bevolkt door Kelten.

kelt.e: Borrowed from Latin Celtae (singular Celta), from Ancient Greek Κελτοί (Keltoí) (singular Κελτός (Keltós)). The singular is first attested in the sixteenth century, but remains rare until the nineteenth century.

s.def: Crete (Greek island in the Mediterranean Sea)
a man-made structure built with materials other than earth and sand, such as bridges, culverts, viaducts, tunnels, etc.

kunstwerk.e: Compound of kunst (“art, capability”) + werk (“work”). Cognate with German Kunstwerk, Swedish konstverk, Danish kunstværk, Norwegian kunstverk.

s.def: captain (person in command of a ship)
Amerindian or Maroon tribal village chief
examples: Ontevreden bewoners van Wit Santi eisen het aftreden van Patrick Mande, kapitein van het dorp, omdat de zittingstermijn van het dorpsbestuur in 2018 is verstreken. Ook zou het dorpshoofd grond hebben verkocht; de opbrengst zou hij voor zichzelf hebben gehouden.

s.def: leader of an ethnic group appointed or recognised by Dutch colonial authorities

kapitein.e: From Middle Dutch capitein, from Old French capitaine, from Medieval Latin capitāneus.

kanon.e: Borrowed from Middle French canon, from Italian cannone.

kwal.e: From earlier qualle; likely an ablaut variant from the same root as kwellen (“to well up, swell up”), whence also kwelder. Cognate with German Qualle.

kelen.e: From Middle Dutch kelen. Equivalent to keel + -en.

s.def: a village and municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

kapelle.e: First attested as infra parochiam de Capella in 1248. Derived from Middle Dutch capelle (“chapel”), whence Dutch kapel.

s.def: clinker brick; (by extension) any kind of brick used for pavement

examples: Het Nederlands heeft vijf basisklinkers: a, e, i, o, u.
De klinkers in het woord "huis" zijn "ui".

s.def: vowel letter (character that designates a vowel)

klinker.e: From earlier klinckaerd, klinkert, from Middle Dutch clinker, clinkaert, related to Middle Low German klinkert (“hard-fired brick”); equivalent to klinken + -er.

s.def: a canon, a cleric subject to an ecclesiastical rule

kanunnik.e: From Middle Dutch canonec, from Ecclesiastical Latin canonicus, from Ancient Greek κανονικός (kanonikós).

kajak.e: Ultimately from Inuktitut ᖃᔭᖅ (qayaq, “man's boat”), from Proto-Eskimo *qyaq.

s.def: knout, a multi-thong scourge as used in tsarist Russia
term of abuse for persons from Westphalia and Northern Germany

knoet.e: Borrowed from Russian кнут (knut), from Old East Slavic кнутъ (knutŭ), from Old Norse knútr (“knot”).

s.def: a male given name, equivalent to English Conrad

koenraad.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch Coenraet, ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *Kōnirād, equivalent to koen (“courageous”) + raad (“counsel”)

examples:
Ik zie, dat op haar zachte koon
Ook frisse lenterozen bloeien,

koon.e: From Middle Dutch coon, from Old Dutch *koun, from Proto-West Germanic *kaun (“jaw, cheek”), from Proto-Germanic *kauną (“jaw, jawbone, cheek”), from Proto-Indo-European *gew- (“to bend, curve”). Cognate with West Frisian koan (“cheek”), Old Norse kaun (“sore, abscess”).

s.def: a changing room (a room in a theatre or other performance venue in which performers may change costumes and apply makeup)
a crude boor, lout, sull; the diminutive often lacks the derogatory connotation
the metallic element and material cobalt
the die made from its oxyde

kobalt.e: Borrowed from German Kobalt, from Kobold, originally an derogatory nickname for the 'inferior' ore which contaminated silver finds.

s.def: anybody living in isolation from society, a recluse, a shut-in

kluizenaar.e: From Middle Dutch clusenâre. Equivalent to kluis + -en- + -aar.

s.def: seal, label from a (quality) control organization
a charter, feudal ordinance or privilege in writing

keur.e: From Middle Dutch core, cuere, from Old Dutch kuri, from Proto-West Germanic *kuʀi.

keu.e: Borrowing from French queue (“tail, queue, cue”).

kleuren.e: From earlier koleuren. By surface analysis, kleur + -en.

examples: Ik ga dit weekend wat klussen in mijn huis.

klonen.e: Borrowed from English clone. Equivalent to kloon + -en.

s.def: contractor who acts as a middle man between prospective employer and prospective employee. Generally for short term employment like day labour. (Archaic)
contractor who deliberately employs staff illegally, so both parties can dodge taxes and cheat social security

koppelbaas.e: From koppelen (“to link, to couple”) + baas (“boss”).

s.def: a ball sack; a bawbag; a scrotum
a bawbag (common term of abuse for a man; a bastard or arsehole, q.v.)
examples: Jij bent met mijn vrouw naar bed geweest, jij klootzak!
Jij kan soms echt een klootzak zijn, weet je.

s.def: a hamlet in Heeze-Leende, North Brabant, Netherlands

kerkhof.e: From Middle Dutch kerchof. Equivalent to kerk (“church”) + hof (“court, garden, yard”).

s.def: din, loud noise made by people
examples: God, wat maken die koters een kabaal.

s.def: commotion, uproar; e.g. much ado about nothing, fuss over nothing
examples: Waar maak je nou zo'n kabaal om, jongen?

kabaal.e: Borrowed from French cabale, (ultimately) from Hebrew קַבָּלָה.

s.def: a rising star, an up-and-coming thing or person, a raging fashion etc.

komeet.e: From Middle Dutch comete, from Old French comete, from Latin comēta, from Ancient Greek κομήτης (komḗtēs).

kalle.e: From Yiddish כּלה (kale, “bride”), from Hebrew כַּלָּה (kalá, “bride”). A relation with the verb kallen (“to chatter”, compare etymology 2 hereunder), claimed in some popular-scientific resources, exists at most through secondary association. Compare German Kalle.

s.def: a turkey; any bird of genus Meleagris, particularly Meleagris gallopavo

kalkoen.e: From older Dutch Calcoensche haan (modern kalkoense haan), from Calcoen, the then-used name for the Indian city Calicut, modern Kozhikode. Although the bird originated in the Americas, confusion arose due to its similarity to the African guinea fowl, which was exported to and imported from India ...

s.def: a character, standardized graphic symbol, such as a letter of an alphabet or a numeric digit
a character; a role, often conventionalised, in theatre
a character, a person's psychological characteristics; especially guts, backbone
a magical mark, a witch's mark

karakter.e: From Middle Dutch caracter, from Middle French caractère, from Old French caractere, from Latin character, from Ancient Greek χαρακτήρ (kharaktḗr).

s.def: to get ready; to finish a task, to be finished
examples: Deze brug is vorige maand eindelijk klaargekomen.

klaarkomen.e: From klaar (“ready”) + komen (“to come”). Compare to and distinguish neatly from German klarkommen (“to manage, to get by”).

s.def: superseded spelling of Congo-Brazzaville: Congo-Brazzaville (synonym of the Republic of the Congo: a country in Central Africa, the smaller of the two countries named Congo)

kikkervisje.e: From kikker (“frog”) + visje (“fish”). Compare West Frisian kikkertsfiskje (“tadpole”).

s.def: cockroach (certain non-termite insect of the order Blattodea)

kakkerlak.e:
First attested as kackerlack towards the end of the 16th century as a derogatory term for a chatterbox or sycophant, deriving from kakker, a contemptuous term derived from kakken (“to shit, defecate”), and lakker (“talker, freeloader”), from likken (“to lick”).
This word was subsequently conflate...

s.def: sister (a nun; a female member of a religious community)

koude.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

koel.e: From Middle Dutch coel, from Old Dutch *kuol, from Proto-West Germanic *kōl(ī), from Proto-Germanic *kōlaz.

s.def: a cold, cool one (e.g. a chilled drink)
to clutch, to apply the clutch

koppelen.e: From Middle Dutch coppelen, which is likely denominative from coppel (modern koppel) and/or directly from Old French cupler. By surface analysis, koppel (“couple”) + -en.

s.def: a piece of chalk (in the diminutive form)

krijt.e: From Middle Dutch crijt, from Latin crēta.

kruid.e: From Middle Dutch cruut (whence also kruit), from Old Dutch krūd, from Proto-West Germanic *krūd.

kampioen.e: From Middle Dutch campioen, from Old Northern French campion, campiun (cf. French champion), from Proto-West Germanic *kampijō (cf. German Kämpe), derived from *kamp (“field, fight”), from Latin campus.

examples: Het nationale voetbalkampioenschap wordt elk jaar gehouden.
Het zwemteam won het regionale kampioenschap met overtuiging.
Na het winnen van het toernooi kreeg hij het kampioenschap en de gouden medaille.
Hij geniet van zijn verdiende kampioenschap en is trots op zijn prestatie.

kampioenschap.e: From kampioen + -schap. First attested in the 19th century.

s.def: a boy chorister, especially a treble whose voice hasn't broken yet
a juvenile acolyte, who assists the officiating priest
an innocent, trustworthy male person, regardless of age
any human male, regardless of age
examples: Wie zei daar domme aap? / Pas op je woorden, knaap

s.def: a big one, whopper, said of a creature or an object

knaap.e: From Middle Dutch cnāpe, from Old Dutch cnapo, from Proto-West Germanic *knappō.

korstmos.e: Compound of korst (“crust”) + mos (“moss”). First attested in the late 18th century.

kopie.e: From Middle Dutch copie, from Old French copie, from Latin copia.

kreet.e: From Middle Dutch crēte, a verbal noun to criten (Dutch krijten). Compare the derivation of beet and bijten.

s.def: to kiss, to give a kiss to
examples: Ik kuste hem zachtjes op zijn wang.
Niemand kust beter dan hij.

kussen.e: From Middle Dutch cussen, from Old Dutch kussen, from Proto-West Germanic *kussijan, from Proto-Germanic *kussijaną.

keuvelen.e: From kevel (“jaw”) + -elen. The -eu- arose due to spontaneous rounding in Hollandic dialects (compare sneven and sneuvelen).

s.def: coolie (person of Indian or Indonesian background)
coolie (Indian Surinamese person, a Surinamese person of Indian descent)

krijsen.e: From Middle Dutch crischen, from Old Dutch *crīscan, from Proto-West Germanic *krīskan, from Proto-Germanic *krīskaną.

ketjap.e: Borrowed from Peranakan Indonesian ketjap. Compare English ketchup, Kapampangan kesiap. Doublet of ketchup.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

keizer.e: From Middle Dutch keiser, from Old Dutch keiser, from Proto-Germanic *kaisaraz, from Latin Caesar. Doublet with tsaar (“tsar”).

s.def: a quarter, one of four (equal) parts

kwart.e: From Middle Dutch quarte, from Old French quart, from Latin quārta.

examples: Dit is de eerste keer dat ik naar Parijs reis.
Ze heeft me al meerdere keren uitgenodigd voor haar verjaardagsfeest.
Na veel tegenslagen hoopten ze op een keer van geluk.
Zijn optreden in de wedstrijd markeerde een belangrijke keer in zijn carrière.

keer.e: From Middle Dutch keer, kere, ultimately a derivative of Dutch keren (“to turn around, revolve”) (compare the semantic development of werf and English turn). Compare German Low German Kehr (“time, turn”), German Kehr (“turn, direction”), Old English ċierr ("turn, time, occasion"; > English chore).

examples: Haar kleinzoon is zwemleraar.

kleinzoon.e: From klein (“small, little”) + zoon (“son”), calque of French petit-fils.

kaneel.e: From Middle Dutch caneel, from Old French canele, a diminutive that ultimately derives from Latin canna (“reed”).

s.def: projectile: bullet, shot (heavy iron ball used for the shot put)

kogel.e: From Middle Dutch coghele, either from or related to Middle High German kugele. If the word is inherited throughout Continental West Germanic, it requires a Proto-West Germanic *kugulā. Further origin uncertain. Possibly related with Proto-Germanic *kuggilaz, whence English cudgel (“heavy stick, ...

s.def: to stick, to be attached glue-like
examples: Ineens merkte ze dat er kauwgom aan haar sandaal kleefde.

s.def: to glue, to adhere something on/to something else, to stick to/on
examples: Zij plaagt haar vader door kauwgum aan zijn snor te kleven.
Deze grondstof kleeft heel erg.

kleven.e: From Middle Dutch clēven, from Old Dutch clivon, from Proto-West Germanic *klibēn, from Proto-Germanic *klibāną, from Proto-Indo-European *gleybʰ- (“to stick”).

s.def: cotton, plant of the genus Gossypium

katoen.e: From Middle Dutch catoen, from older cotoen, from Old French coton, from Old Italian cotone, from Arabic قُطُن (quṭun).

examples: De deur stond op een kier

s.def: a child born on Christmas (Eve or Day)
a dupe, as easy to trick as a young child
a calf (young cow or bull; young bovine)
a calf (young elephant; young deer; young of certain marine mammals)

kalf.e: From Middle Dutch calf, from Old Dutch calf, from Proto-Germanic *kalbaz.

kwadraat.e: Borrowed from Middle French quadrat, from Latin quadrātum.

s.def: a paederast, a pedophile who sexually abuses boys
rascal boy, an imp particularly inclined to mischief
examples: Ach, hij is een kwajongen, maar dat hoort erbij.

s.def: turnip (the white root of a yellow-flowered plant, Brassica rapa, grown as a vegetable and as fodder for cattle)
petty in relation to pain or hardship, with a low pain threshold, with little endurance
to mess, to screw around (to waste time)
lemonade or another citrus-based beverage; a drink made of lemon juice, water and optionally sugar (can denote both cold and hot drinks)
examples: Op deze bloedhete dag was het heerlijk eens kwast te drinken.

kwast.e: From Middle Dutch quast, from Old Dutch *kwast, from Proto-Germanic *kwastuz.

s.def: Christmas, the major Christian feast commemorating the birth of Jesus Christ

kerst.e: From Middle Dutch Kerst, from Latin Christus (“Christ”).

s.def: a border, seem, edge, fringe etc.

kust.e: From Middle Dutch cost, from Old French coste, from Latin costa (“rib, side”), cognate with English coast, German Küste, French côte etc.

s.def: coolness, a cold, but not freezing temperature, possibly pleasant
a breeze; any kind of wind
examples: Er stak een harde koelte op.

koelte.e: From Middle Dutch coelde. Equivalent to koel + -te.

s.def: inflection of kortst, the superlative degree of kort:
inflection of kortst, the superlative degree of kort:
inflection of kortst, the superlative degree of kort:
inflection of kwaadst, the superlative degree of kwaad:
inflection of kwaadst, the superlative degree of kwaad:
inflection of kwaadst, the superlative degree of kwaad:
inflection of koudst, the superlative degree of koud:
inflection of koudst, the superlative degree of koud:
inflection of koudst, the superlative degree of koud:
inflection of kleinst, the superlative degree of klein:
inflection of kleinst, the superlative degree of klein:
inflection of kleinst, the superlative degree of klein:
to clap (make sound or music with the hands)
to applaud (clap hands in order to praise)
to smack, strike rather hard and/or loud
to crack with a loud sound, like a whip
to wag one's lips, talk, converse
examples: Men gaf hem, boven dien, waerschynlyk zynen naem van Garrulus, omdat hy, in eene kooi opgevoed, zeer wel leert klappen, gelyk de Pappegaeyen.
Yo, wil je straks jonko klappen?

klappen.e: From Middle Dutch clappen, from Old Dutch *klappon, from Proto-West Germanic *klappōn.

s.def: to coo like a pigeon, speak in a soft and low voice
to fish by pulling a small funnel-shaped dragnet by a long rope over the sea floor close to the shoreline or near shallows
calm, tranquil, free of noise and disturbance

kalm.e: From earlier calm, from French calme.

s.def: waiter (a server in a restaurant or similar)
examples: De kelner bracht onze drankjes.
Kun je de kelner vragen om de rekening?

s.def: jack (mechanical device for lifting heavy machinery)

krik.e: Borrowed from French cric, from Middle French cric, from Middle High German kriec (“jack, winch”).

kubus.e: From Latin cubus, from Ancient Greek κύβος (kúbos).

s.def: Kyrgyz (of, from, or pertaining to Kyrgyzstan, the Kyrgyz people or the Kyrgyz language)

kristal.e: From Middle Dutch cristal, kerstal, from Old French cristal, from Latin crystallum, from Ancient Greek κρύσταλλος (krústallos).

kwestie.e: From Middle Dutch questie, from Latin quaestiō or perhaps via Old French question (also from Latin).

s.def: the English Channel or Strait of Dover, the North Sea strait between England and France

kanaal.e: From Middle Dutch canael, from older canel, from Old French canal, from Latin canālis (“pipe, channel, canal”), from canna (“reed, cane”), from Ancient Greek κάννα (kánna, “reed”), from Akkadian 𒄀 (qanû, “reed”), from Sumerian 𒄀𒈾 (gi.na).

s.def: a rope, cord (length of twisted strands)
examples: Ik heb een koord nodig om dit pakket te binden.
Ze droeg een ketting aan een dun koordje.

koord.e: From Middle Dutch corde, coorde, from Old French corde, from Latin chorda, from Ancient Greek χορδή (khordḗ, “string of gut, the string of a lyre”). Doublet of koorde.

s.def: crossword, a word puzzle arranged in rows and columns
crossword, a word puzzle arranged in rows and columns
view, opinion (way of understanding or looking at something)
examples: Na mijn wereldreis heb ik een heel andere kijk op de wereld.

komedie.e: From Latin cōmoedia, from Ancient Greek κωμῳδία (kōmōidía).

kalk.e: From Middle Dutch calc, from Old Dutch *kalk, from Proto-West Germanic *kalk.

s.def: to comb (use a comb to groom hair, fur etc.)
to use any other comb, even mechanically

kammen.e: From kam + -en. Displaced earlier kemmen (see there for further etymology), which is morphologically identical to kammen but was inherited from Proto-Germanic, demonstrating the effects of umlaut.

s.def: a cadaver, carcass, corpse, especially when in a state of decay; in particular an animal corpse

examples: Zijn schoonmoeder is echt een kreng.
Ik probeer het blik open te krijgen, maar het kreng werkt niet mee.

kreng.e: From Middle Dutch krenge, from Old French carogne (“carrion”), cognate of English crone.

s.def: a bruise, a physically hurt part of the body (notably skin) which has no open wound

kneuzen.e: From Middle Dutch cnuesen, from Old Dutch *cnusen, from Proto-West Germanic *knussjan.

s.def: a harrier, predatory bird of the subfamily Circinae
a chicken thief, who steals fowl
examples: Zoo gingen beiden nu op de vaart, en Reinaert bracht Tibert in de schuur van eenen pastoor, waaruit hij daags te voren eenen haan had geroofd. Een strik stond daar gereed gespannen om den kiekendief te vangen.
“ Padopen ! ” zei Jan, “ daar loopt de vos met mijnen schoonsten haan eweg! ” Hij speelt zijn klonen uit en op zijn slobben met den vlegel in de hand, vliegt hij den kiekendief achterna.

s.def: to scratch on a hard, even surface
to make a similar sound, like certain voices grate and birds caw, croak, hoot or screech

krassen.e:
From Middle Dutch crassen, probably from Middle High German kratzen, from Old High German krazzōn, from Proto-Germanic *krattōną, presumably of imitative origin, but compare Albanian gërresë.
Cognate with modern German kratzen, Middle Low German kratten. From a variant *kratjaną stem Middle Dutch...

s.def: choice, whether already made or not
examples: Je mag een drank naar keuze nemen.
Je moet een keuze maken tussen deze twee mogelijkheden.

keuze.e: From Middle Dutch cose. Formed from core (modern keur) by analogy with kiesen.

klad.e: From Middle Dutch kladde. Compare German Kladde.

s.def: someone who knocks or drops by a lot
agreement, particularly concerning the release of prisoners

kartel.e: Borrowed from German Kartell in its current senses; the now-obsolete sense "treaty" was borrowed directly from French cartel.

kelder.e: From Middle Dutch kelre, kellere, from Old Dutch *kellari, from Late Latin cellarium.

s.def: chain (a series of interconnected rings or links)

ketting.e: From Middle Dutch kettinc, from the more common form ketene, from Old Dutch *ketinna, from ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *katinnjā, from Vulgar Latin *catenia, from Latin catēna.

s.def: boarding school, where pupils get lessons, lodgings and meals
to churn (agitate milk rapidly to obtain butter)

karnen.e: From Middle Dutch carnen, kernen, from Old Dutch [Term?], from Proto-West Germanic [Term?], from Proto-Germanic *kirnijaną.

s.def: to crown, adorn with a coronet, wreath etc.
to acclaim, hail, acknowledge ... a winner, champion etc.

kronen.e: From Middle Dutch cronen. Equivalent to kroon + -en.

examples: "Help me!" kreunde de gevallen motorrijder.

s.def: to get a kick out of
kick (remove someone from an online activity)
tailor (person who makes, repairs, or alters clothing as profession)

kleermaker.e: From Middle Dutch cledermakere with loss of intervocalic -d-. Equivalent to kleer (singular of plurale tantum kleren) + maker.

s.def: A crane; a bird of the Gruidae family.

examples: Mark en Elizabeth vormen een leuk koppel.
Deze Audi heeft een koppel van 530 Nm.

koppel.e: From Middle Dutch coppel, from Old French cople. Doublet of copula.

s.def: babbler, chatterbox, all too talkative person
a poppy, any of several plants of genus Papaver
the frail flower of the poppy

klaproos.e: From older klapperroos, borrowed from German Klapperrose. Equivalent to klappen (“to pop, bang”) + roos (“rose”).

knijpkat.e: From knijpen (“to pinch, to squeeze”) + kat (“cat”), probably named so because the sound of the spinning dynamo sounds somewhat like a cat.

s.def: cat-like, belonging to the Felidae family

kerstfeest.e: From Kerst (“Christ”) + feest (“feast, festival”). Compare German Christfest.

s.def: canoe (a small long and narrow boat)
examples: We peddelden met de kano over de rustige rivier.
De kinderen vonden het leuk om in de kano's te varen tijdens het zomerkamp.

kano.e: Borrowed from Spanish canoa, from Taíno *kanowa. The current spelling and pronunciation is likely influenced by French canot.

kerker.e: From Middle Dutch kerker, from Old Dutch *karkari, from Latin carcer (“prison, jail”). Compare German Kerker, Gothic 𐌺𐌰𐍂𐌺𐌰𐍂𐌰 (karkara).

examples: Kinderen, ga in een kring staan!

s.def: circle, clique (a community of people with a shared common interest)
examples: Dit is in deze kringen ongebruikelijk.

s.def: a circular stain left on a table by a wet glass
the student organization of a university faculty

kring.e: From Middle Dutch kring, from Old Dutch *kring, from Proto-Germanic *kringaz.

s.def: village (a Southeast Asian rural habitation of size between a hamlet and a town); kampung
a quarter in a Southeast Asian city; an Indonesian quarter

examples: While the practices of Kabbala were known in Europe from very early times, the expulsion of Spain's Jews in 1492 resulted in a great influx of Kabbalic lore into the Latin kingdoms.

s.def: Kashubian (Slavic language spoken in the Pomeranian region of Poland)
Kashubian related to the Kashubian language

examples: Ik heb al de hele dag barstende koppijn.

s.def: a musical and/or comedic kind of theatre such as cabaret

koepel.e: Borrowed from Italian cupola. The variants were borrowed from or influenced by French.

s.def: short-skirted or wearing a short skirt
a surname originating as an occupation

krijg.e: From Middle Dutch crijch, from Old Dutch *krīg, from Proto-West Germanic *krīg. Cognate to Old High German krig, Middle High German kriec, German Krieg, Old Saxon *krīg, Middle Low German krîch, German Low German Krieg, Old Frisian *krīch, West Frisian kriich.

kruisigen.e: From Old Dutch krucigon, from Proto-West Germanic *krūcigōn, from Latin cruciō.

s.def: to strike with the head; as in soccer, to head (the ball)
examples: De voetballer kopte de bal direct binnen.
De krant kopte "Meer vertrouwen in de economie".
Als je je petunia's kopt, zullen ze er langer goed uitzien.

s.def: a defeat or punishment, especially a heavy defeat

an unmarried woman who has dedicated her life to service to the Roman Catholic Church without entering a monastery, now especially denoting a laywoman in the Netherlands before the Roman Catholic emancipation

klop.e: From Middle Dutch clop. Equivalent to a deverbal from kloppen.

s.def: nursery school (UK) or kindergarten (USA); usually for children aged 3 to 6
to chop, as with an axe
to cut down, fell (e.g. a tree)
to fit with a hairstyle or headdress
to fit with a hairstyle or headdress

kappen.e: From Middle Dutch cappen. Further origin unsettled. Apparently related to German Low German kappen (“to clip, cut”), German kappen (“to clip, cut”), English chap and chop; thus it would also be distantly related to tjappen. The slang sense may have a distinct (unknown) etymology.

s.def: to camp, camp put, encamp, go camping
examples: We kampeerden in de bergen tijdens onze vakantie.
Ze kampeert graag in het bos.

s.def: a cookbook, a recipe book (book of culinary recipes)
Expression of disgust, as if one has to vomit.
rock or cliff in or around sea water
rock on land, e.g. used as a habitat by animals

klip.e: From Middle Dutch clippe, from Old Dutch *klippa, from Proto-Germanic *klippō. See klif (“cliff”), which probably shares the same ultimate origin.

s.def: a non-believer or an infidel from the Muslim viewpoint
a black person, notably a (Southern African) Bantu; (Suriname, offensive, ethnic slur) an Afro-Surinamese person

kaffer.e: Borrowed from Portuguese cafre, from Arabic كَافِر (kāfir, “infidel”).

s.def: an official carriage (type of horse-drawn vehicle)

karos.e: Borrowed from French carrosse, from Italian carrozza, a derivation from carro, from Latin carrus. Doublet of kar.

klap.e: From Middle Dutch clap. Equivalent to a deverbal from klappen.

s.def: tub, basin (medium to large container, e.g. used for washing)
tub, container (small container, e.g. used for containing butter or spreads)

kuip.e: From Middle Dutch cupe, from Latin cūpa.

kriebelen.e: Likely an alteration of kribbelen, frequentative of kribben (“to scratch”), which is likely a by-form of the more common verb krabben (“to scratch”).

s.def: a grain, a pellet (particle of a granular substance)

korrel.e: From Middle Dutch corle, from Old Dutch [Term?]. Equivalent to koren + -el.

s.def: a frill of feathers or skin of a bird or reptile

kraag.e: From Middle Dutch crage, from Proto-Germanic *kragan- (“throat”), probably from Proto-Indo-European *gʷrogʰ-, which could be related to Ancient Greek βρόχθος (brókhthos, “throat”).

krabben.e: From Middle Dutch crabben, further from Proto-Germanic *krabbōną, with a geminated variant of the stem in Proto-Germanic *krabitaz, whence Dutch kreeft (“lobster”).

knopen.e: From Middle Dutch cnopen, from cnoop (modern knoop).

karaat.e: From the Greek word keration (fruit of the carob tree), which came into Dutch via Arabic and Italian. The seeds of such fruit were used in the ancient world as units for measuring weight.

kloven.e: From Middle Dutch cloven, causative form of Middle Dutch clieven, from Old Dutch *clievan, from Proto-Germanic *kleubaną. Akin to English cleave.

s.def: Elector, Prince-Elector (of the Holy Roman Empire)

keurvorst.e: From Middle Dutch coorvorst, likely from Middle High German [Term?]. Equivalent to keur + vorst.

kollet.e: From Middle French culet, diminutive of cul (“bottom”), itself from Latin culus (“arse”).

s.def: nonstandard form of abbreviation of kilometer per uur

examples: krasse knar

kladden.e: From Middle Dutch cladden. By surface analysis, klad + -en.

s.def: to annoy, to be a nuisance
to work one's crutches, e.g. in order to pull oneself up from the ground
petting zoo (educational and recreational facility for familiarising children with (chiefly) farm animals)
base (e.g. a fire station, or an army base, etc.)

kazerne.e: Borrowed from French caserne, ultimately from Latin quaternus.

s.def: to acquit oneself (of some job or task)

kwijten.e: From Middle Dutch quiten, from Old French quiter, from Latin quiētāre.

s.def: cadre, member of the core group of a political organization
examples: de gestaalde kaders van de communistische partij

s.def: the cadres of a political organization, collectively
examples: het kader stond achter de leider

kader.e: From French cadre, from Italian quadro (“framed painting, square”), from Latin quadrum, from quattuor (“four”).

s.def: pet name, name of endearment, hypocorism

koosnaam.e: Borrowed from German Kosename. By surface analysis, kozen (“caress”) + naam (“name”).

examples: met sint-juttemis als de kalveren op het ijs dansen

s.def: a (now usually benevolent) tiny folkloric bearded man who traditionally wears a pointy red hat, lives in harmony with nature and resides in mushrooms or trees, similar to a gnome, leprechaun or brownie
a very young female member of the scout movement

kabouter.e: Possibly already from Middle Dutch. Equivalent to a compound of koof (from Middle Dutch kove, from Old Dutch *kovo, from Proto-Germanic *kubô (“hut, shed”)) and a less certain second element that might be hou (“friendly, favorable disposition”). Related to German Kobold. For sense 2, compare with...

klastisch.e: From Ancient Greek κλᾰστός (klăstós) + -isch, from κλάσειν (klásein, “to break”).

kruidenier.e: From Middle Dutch crudenare, with later change of suffix to -ier. Equivalent to kruid + -en- + -ier.

kwaal.e: From Middle Dutch quâle, from Old Dutch *kwāla, from Proto-West Germanic *kwālu, from Proto-Germanic *kwēlō.

s.def: the sweet mixture caramel, used for confection
a piece (usually block-shaped) of candy made with it

karamel.e: From French caramel, from Spanish caramelo, from Latin canamella (“sugar cane”).

s.def: small round bun, usually used in the diminutive form

kaliber.e: From French calibre (“bore of a gun, size, capacity (literally, and figuratively), also weight”). Origin uncertain, perhaps, from Latin qua libra (“of what dimensions, weight”).

kwarts.e: Borrowed from German Quarz, from Middle High German twarc, probably from a West Slavic language, from Proto-Slavic *tvьrdъ.

s.def: cycle (iterative sequential process consisting of more than one stage)
one who cracks or an instrument used to crack

examples: Hij moet voor een onderzoek naar de kraker.
In de oude fabriek wonen nu stonede krakers.

s.def: a thing of good quality, a cracker
examples: Wij verwachten morgen een echte kraker in Port Elizabeth.

s.def: supporter (someone who is a fan of a certain sports team or sportsperson)
examples: Maar ondanks het aanvuren van de vele Dyompo-krakers en de medewerking van de bomen, ging Wind als eerste langs de laatste controlepost.
De achtste kruistocht eindigde zonder veel succes.
De kruistochten hadden een diepe invloed op de middeleeuwse geschiedenis.

s.def: quest, 'sacred' cause, notably against some evil
examples: Hij is op een persoonlijke kruistocht tegen corruptie.

s.def: naval crossing, act of crossing a body of water

kruistocht.e: From kruis (“cross”) + tocht (“journey”). First attested in 1700.

s.def: crusader (a fighter in the mediaeval crusades)
examples: De kruisvaarders trokken naar het Heilige Land.
Hij bestudeert de geschiedenis van de kruisvaarders.

s.def: a fair (e.g. outdoor fair, village fair, county fair)

kermis.e: From Middle Dutch kermisse. Equivalent to a contraction of kerkmis = kerk + mis. Compare the same in German Kirmes.

kwikzilver.e: From Middle Dutch quicsilver, calque of Latin argentum vivum. Equivalent to kwik (“quick”) + zilver (“silver”).

examples: Een lijger is een hybride kruising tussen een mannetjesleeuw en een vrouwtjestijger.

kronkelen.e: From Middle Dutch cronkelen, from Proto-Germanic *krunkōną.

kruiden.e: From Middle Dutch cruden. Equivalent to kruid + -en.

kwelder.e: From kwellen (“to rise”) + -er. Cognate with German Quelle (“spring, well, source”).

s.def: a calyx (outermost whorl of flower parts)

kelk.e: From Middle Dutch kelc, from Old Dutch kelik, from Latin calix, calyx, from Ancient Greek κᾰ́λῠξ (kắlŭx).

s.def: someone suffering from a skin disease or glandular swelling

klier.e: From Middle Dutch cliere. Cognate with Scots clyre (“tumour”).

kiem.e: From Middle Dutch kieme, kyme. Although the word is found as Old Dutch kīmo, it was probably borrowed from Middle Low German kīme, from Old Saxon kīmo because it did not undergo the regular change of -ī- to -ij- (so that *kijm would be expected). Possible influence of Latin cyma is also possible....

s.def: couch grass, quackgrass, quitch (Elymus repens syn. Elytrigia repens)

kweek.e: From Middle Dutch queke, from Old Dutch *kwika, from Proto-West Germanic *kwikwā, from Proto-Germanic *kwikwǭ. Compare German Quecke and English quitch.

kinderwagen.e: Compound of kind (“child”) + -er- + wagen (“wagon, car(t), vehicle”).

karwei.e: From Middle Dutch corweide, from Old French corvee, from Late Latin conrogāta. Doublet of corvee.

s.def: small children hear everything (literally: small pots have big ears/handles)

examples: Zij hebben twee kleindochters.

kleindochter.e: From klein (“small”) + dochter (“daughter”), calque of French petite-fille.

kwijnen.e: From Middle Dutch quinen, from Old Dutch quīnan, from Proto-Germanic *kwīnaną (Proto-West Germanic *kwīnan).

examples: In de kerk hing een groot kruisbeeld boven het altaar.

kosmopolitisch.e: Probably borrowed from German kosmopolitisch. Equivalent to kosmopoliet + -isch.

kerkvoogd.e: From Middle Dutch kerkvoochd. Equivalent to kerk + voogd.

kerselaar.e: From kers + -e- + -aar, with the -l- perhaps by analogy with hazelaar.

kalligrafie.e: From Ancient Greek καλλιγραφία (kalligraphía, “pretty writing”), from κάλλος (kállos, “beauty”) + γράφω (gráfo, “to draw”).

kaduuk.e: Borrowed from Middle French caduc, from Latin cadūcus.

knijf.e: From Middle Dutch cnijf, from Old Dutch *knīf, from Proto-Germanic *knībaz. Related to Dutch knijpen (“to pinch”). Cognate with English knife, Low German Knief, Luxembourgish Knäip, Swedish kniv, Old Norse knífr.

knokkel.e: From Middle Dutch cnokel, from Old Dutch *knukil, from Proto-West Germanic *knukil, from Proto-Germanic *knukilaz, a diminutive of *knukô (“bone”) (whence knook). klepel.e: From Middle Dutch clepel, cleppel, from Old Dutch *klepil, *kleppil, from Proto-Germanic *klapilaz, *klappilaz. Equivalent to klappen or kleppen + -el.
