1909 January 28, advertisement, Zierikzeesche Nieuwsbode, vol. 65, no. 8726, page 4.
Verloren: Knalpot Motorrijwiel, tusschen Duivendijke en Kerkwerve. Terugbezorging tegen belooning bij I. DE BROEKERT, Zierikzee.
Lost: Silencer of motorcycle, between Duivendijke and Kerkwerve. Return for reward at I. DE BROEKERT, Zierikzee.

knalpot.e: From knal or knallen (“to bang, to make a loud, sharp noise”) + pot (“pot”).

s.def: square cobbles used for paving streets; sampietrini
a square cobblestone as used for paving streets; a sampietrino
a child's head or representation thereof (as a pendant for example)

kouter.e: From Middle Dutch couter, colter, from Latin culter (“knife, razor”).

s.def: a pin used for fastening, a peg
a rope used to tie something
a moustache covering much of the area above the upper lip, e.g. a handlebar moustache or a walrus moustache

examples: Is dan dat monster, dat op gindsche grafzark zich de knevels likt, niet uw zoon?

kegelsnede.e: From kegel + snede, calque of Latin sectio conica. Coined by Simon Stevin in the spelling keghelsne.

s.def: kompromat, blackmail material, dirt (chiefly in Russian contexts)
caravan (a convoy or procession of travelers, their vehicles and cargo, and any pack animals, especially camels crossing a desert)

karavaan.e: Borrowing from French caravane, replacing forms derived from Middle Dutch carvane.

kajuit.e: From Middle Dutch kayhute, of uncertain ultimate origin; possibly borrowed from Old French. According to Watkins, possibly a mix of cabane + hutte. Cognate with German Kajüte (from Middle Low German kajüte), French cahute.

s.def: big house, (used to be) inhabited by big traders (in coffee for example)
bag to keep artificial fertilizer in

kindertjes.e: Formed as the plural diminutive of kinderen, the standard but irregular plural of kind, as if the latter was the regular plural of the (nonexistent) singular kinder. The standard plural of the diminutive of kind is kindjes, but this alternative form is common.

s.def: fireworks that primarily create bangs (as opposed to creating a visual display)
a crime fiction or a crime series, specifically a German-language one

kokako.e: Borrowed from English kokako, from Māori kōkako.

s.def: keyboard (set of keys to operate a musical instrument)

klavier.e: From Middle Dutch klavier, from Middle French clavier.

klager.e: From Middle Dutch clager (“accuser, plaintiff”). Equivalent to klagen + -er.

kauwgom.e: From kauwen + gom, likely a calque of English chewing gum.

s.def: an art expert with significant influence on what is generally considered (high) art
examples: Overleden kunstpaus Jan Hoet had West-Vlaamse roots.

s.def: a cricket, insect of the family Gryllidae

kriek.e: From Middle Dutch crieke f, from Old Dutch *krieka, unexplained; perhaps from Late Latin prunum graecum (“Greek plum”), though this isn't attested until 1517 in a Latin-German dictionary. Latin gr was often derived as kr in older Germanic borrowings (see Proto-West Germanic *Krēkō (“Greek”)). See...

examples: Zal sebiet is die papaja kaleren, zie!

s.def: column, i.e. vertical line of entries in a table
column, i.e. vertical body of text

kolom.e: From Middle Dutch colomme, from Old French columne, from Latin columna (“a column, pillar, post”), originally a collateral form of columen, contraction culmen (“a pillar, top, crown, summit”). Doublet of column and colonne.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Noord-Beveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

kats.e: First attested as chats in 1204. Etymology unknown. Perhaps related to dialectal kats (“sludge, dirty gunk”). No relationship with dialectal kets (“road used to bring cattle to pasture”) or kade (“embankment”). Compare Kaatsheuvel.

s.def: a mold in which Edam cheese is pressed

klojo.e: From klooien, klootzak, kloot or a related word.

s.def: a head with lots of curls
a head with lots of curls

kwartel.e: From Middle Dutch quattel, quattele, from Old Dutch *kwahtila, from Proto-West Germanic *hwahtlā.

s.def: a small, unimportant and rather wet country, virtually exclusively said of the Netherlands
examples: Ik had niet gedacht dat ze in ons kikkerlandje zo goede films konden maken.

synonym of herik (“charlock”) (Rhamphospermum arvense, syns. Sinapis arvensis, etc.)

krodde.e: From Middle Dutch krode, from Old Dutch *kroda, Proto-West Germanic *krodu. Cognate to German Kröte (“toad”).

s.def: to enjoy the evening, to idle, to relax (during the evening)
examples:
M. C. Piepers, "Over de Nederlansche taal in Oost-Indië", De gids, 39 (1875), 3, September, 450.
De voor- of achtergalerij is veelal de plaats waar de oudgast zich overgeeft aan het klimaatschieten.
The front or back gallery is usually the locale where the longtimer yields to idling.
Klimaatschieten is overigens in gemakkelijke houding, de dagelijksche zorgen zooveel mogelijk uit het hoofd zettende, den koelen avond genieten.
"Klimaatschieten" by the way means to enjoy the cool evening, trying to ignore everyday worries as much as possible.

klimaatschieten.e: Originates from Dutch spoken on the Dutch East Indies. Compound of klimaat + schieten.

s.def: Calais, a city in the North of France

examples: Het Kanaalplan behelst een versterking van de werkgelegenheid en de economie voor de Brusselaars die wonen in de Kanaalbuurt.

s.def: basket for the clothes of a baby

examples: een kregelig antwoord
een kregelige bui

kobbe.e: Uncertain. Perhaps referring to an animal with a thick, round body, in which probably from the same source as Old Norse kobbi (“seal”), which see. Compare English cob (“male swan”) and dialectal German Koppe (“crow”).

s.def: a playing card of the diamonds suit
chewing on one's own teeth (especially after having taken drugs)
a playing card of the clubs suit

knibbelen.e: From Middle Dutch knibbelen. A variant of knabbelen, influenced by kibbelen.

kibboets.e: From Hebrew קִבּוּץ, a pi'el construction of the root ק־ב־ץ.

examples:
1991, KVK, "Wakker met een wijsje".
Hoe deftiger de wagen, hoe knorriger de kop.

s.def: a coffee bean, a seed of a plant of the genus Coffea
coffee time: time for a coffee break

examples: Ik gebruik hier gemaks- en kortheidshalve de term 'welstand' voor zorgvuldige aandacht voor esthetische schoonheid, vormgeving of ruimtelijke kwaliteit zoals aan de orde bij ruimtelijke ordening, stedenbouwkunde, architectuur, planologie en ontwerpen openbare ruimte.

kindertje.e: Back-formation as a singular form of the plural diminutive kindertjes.

s.def: Children and Family Court Advisory and Support Service: Dutch national board for control on child welfare

examples: De overheid investeert in programma's voor kinderzorg om het welzijn van kwetsbare kinderen te verbeteren.

Het waren twee koninghs kindren,
 Sy hadden malkander soo lief;

kattin.e: From Middle Dutch cattinne, equivalent to kat + -in. Compare German Kätzin.

koffiezetapparaat.e: From koffie (“coffee”) + zetten (“to make, to prepare”) + apparaat (“machine, device”).

s.def: butterflyfish, fish of the family Chaetodontidae

klipvis.e: Compound of klip + vis. Perhaps a calque.

examples: Twee meeuwen klapwieken krijsend over de hoofden van de journalisten, vechtend om een overgebleven frietje.
Zo maken grote vogels – zoals vale gieren – slim gebruik van thermiek om hoogte te winnen zonder hun vleugels te hoeven klapwieken.

s.def: to alternate, to undulate, usually shifting rapidly between extremes
examples: Kortom, de emoties klapwieken heen en weer tussen jankende pret en huilend plezier!

klapwieken.e: Compound of klappen (“to clap, to flap”) + wieken (“to use wings”).

s.def: a document treated in (any of the houses of) parliament
a short play suitable for being performed in a room or other small venue with a small cast

examples: Het aangekochte geschut varieerde van kleine steen— en kamerstukken van 600 pond gewicht, die kogels van 1 '/. pond schoten, tot de grote stukken van 6.000 pond gewicht voor kogels van 38 pond.

kamerstuk.e: From kamer (“chamber (of parliament); room”) + stuk (“document, piece”).

s.def: alternative form of kindeken; diminutive of kind

koenheid.e: From koen + -heid. Cognate with German Kühnheit.

kerktoren.e: From Middle Dutch kerctoren. Equivalent to kerk + toren.

koeterwaals.e: From koeterwaal + -s, or simply borrowed from German Kauderwelsch.

kamille.e: From Middle Dutch camille, from older camomille, from Latin camomilla, from Ancient Greek χαμαίμηλον (khamaímēlon). The shortened form (ca. 1460) was probably influenced by German Kamille (before 1350).

s.def: something or someone who is big or the biggest of their kind; a whopper
one who is admirable; a hero, a star
examples: Wauw, wat een kanjer!

kanjer.e: From Middle Dutch keniart, canjaert, kangart, kenjard, from Old French cagnard.

s.def: a small piece of hard (chiefly animal) feces

keutel.e: From Middle Dutch cotel, cuetel. Probably a derivative of koot (“little bone”), thus originally “something hard”, with the suffix -el. Cognate with German Köttel.

kondigen.e: From Middle Dutch condigen, from condich, from cont (modern kond). By surface analysis, kondig + -en or kond + -igen.

kond.e: From Middle Dutch cont, from Old Dutch cunt, from Proto-Germanic *kunþaz.

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

koum.e: First attested as cougghum in 1410. Borrowed from West Frisian Koum, potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Coda (a personal name) or Côfa and hēm (“home, settlement”).

kuim.e: From Middle Dutch cume (“barely; almost”), from Old Dutch, from Proto-Germanic *kūma- (“pitiful, dear”). Cognate to German kaum (“barely”) and come in English comely.

s.def: a mother who neglects or mistreats her children; a bad mother
something or someone undersized or puny

knechtschap.e: From Middle Dutch cnechtscap. Equivalent to knecht (“servant”) + -schap (“-ship, -hood”).

s.def: chickpea, a seed of Cicer arietinum

kikkererwt.e: Ultimately from Latin cicer. Reanalysed as kikker + erwt.

koeienvlaai.e: From koe (“cow”) + vlaai (“a type of flat fruit pie”). Named after the typical shape of the excrement.

s.def: exploding cigar (mainly as a stereotypical practical joke)

klapsigaar.e: Compound of klappen (“to explode, to burst”) + sigaar (“cigar”).

krenken.e: From Middle Dutch crenken, crinken; related to krank (“sick, ill”). Equivalent to krank (“sick, ill”) + -en (“deadjectival”) and shows the effect of historical umlaut. Cognate with German kränken.

s.def: a government that is in-fighting on a regular basis

kibbelkabinet.e: Neologism. From kibbelen (“to squabble”) + kabinet (“administration; government”).

examples: Ik denk dat het mengsel van byzantinisme en kinnesinne, waarmee de Londense literaire wereld hem tegemoet kwam, Eliot in zijn schulp heeft doen kruipen.
Een Apeldoornse wanhoopsdaad, ingegeven door frustratie en kinnesinne.

kinnesinne.e: Borrowed from Yiddish קנאה ושׂנאה (kine vesine) or קנאה־שׂנאה (kine-sine), from Hebrew קִנְאָה וְשִׂנְאָה (kin'á v'sin'á).

krachttoer.e: Compound of kracht + toer, calque of French tour de force.

s.def: catnip, catwort, any plant of the genus Nepeta
a hamlet in Meerssen, Limburg, Netherlands

kasen.e:
First attested as caswyn in 1409. Etymology uncertain. Proposed etymologies include a derivation from the plural form of Latin casa (“cottage, house”) or a compound of Vulgar Latin *cassanus (“oak, Quercus sp.”) and Middle Dutch winne (“leased farmstead, farm”).
See also Limburgish Kaze.

s.def: audience whose only purpose is to applaud
sheep and goats (sometimes also including pigs, fowl or even bees); defined as smaller-sized livestock
examples: Het kleinvee graast dichtbij de boerderij.
Ze hebben voornamelijk kleinvee, zoals schapen en geiten.

kleinvee.e: From klein + vee. Compare German Kleinvieh, French menu bétail, African English smallstock.

s.def: person who is often/easily cold, person bothered by the cold a lot

examples: Salad of red beetroots.

karoot.e: Borrowed from French carote, variant of carotte.

examples: Hij hield zijn hand omhoog, waarin de paarse bollen van rode kroten aan hun lange, rode uitlopers bungelden, vers van de grond.
Scholtmeijer stelt zich voor hoe een huisvrouw bij de groenteboer vroeg om wortels en rode bieten, terwijl de groenteboer alleen weet had van peentjes en kroten.

kroot.e: Borrowed from French carote, variant of carotte.

kolderstok.e: Compound of kolder + stok. The etymology of the first element is uncertain.

s.def: to express breast milk outside of breastfeeding, for later use

kolven.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

s.def: celebrity criminal, a criminal with persistently favourable media coverage
climate criminal, someone whose actions with respect to the climate, in particular climate change, are believed to be criminal or, when used as hyperbole, outrageous
examples: Op een persconferentie maakte hij Abbott ten overvloede uit voor 'klimaatcrimineel', waardoor het niet lang duurde voordat Greg Hunt, minister van Milieu, Bandt ervan betichtte politiek voordeel te willen behalen uit de ontwrichtende ramp waarop de brand ook toen al leek uit te draaien.
‘Die mensen achter al die glazen façades’, besluit Sabido de tour, ‘zijn klimaatcriminelen. Die moeten we zo ver mogelijk bij onze politici vandaan houden.’

klimaatgevoeligheid.e: From klimaat (“climate”) + gevoeligheid (“sensitivity”), calque of English climate sensitivity.

kopstem.e: From kop + stem, calque of German Kopfstimme.

s.def: Katenspeck, a hot-steamed type of bacon

katenspek.e: Borrowed from German Katenspeck (literally “hut bacon”), from the cognates of Dutch kot + spek.

s.def: hat of a kabouter, a (usually red) conical hat (resembling a Phrygian cap or a wizard's hat)
shallow children's pool (at a swimming pool)
an unemotional or empathyless person; a cold fish

examples: Hoe smaakt jouw kippenborst?
Ik heb helemaal geen kippenborst, man! Kom niet bij mijn borst.

s.def: glass bell jar for storing cheese, akin to a cheese dish
an institution or situation that is considered to be nasty or corrupt (nearly always in reference to The Hague, so often modified by "Haagse")
a breakwater, usually in a river or other stream
a stall, a compartment in a stable

kribbe.e: From Middle Dutch cribbe, from Old Dutch kribba, from Frankish *kribbija, from Proto-Germanic *kribjǭ. Doublet of crèche.

s.def: a dam or breakwater constructed perpendicular to the bank, strand and/or water current, to prevent erosion

kinderoppas.e: From kinder (“child”, compound form of kind) + oppas (“supervision, babysitter”).

koste wat kost.e: Calque of French coûte que coûte, which is also used in Dutch texts.

s.def: type of pseudomedicine that uncritically alleges psychosomatic causes and blames the patient for diseases

kwakdenken.e: Coined by Karin Spaink in 1992. Blend of kwakzalverij (“quackery”) + denken (“thought, thinking”).

s.def: assimilation of carbon dioxide (by plants and algae), photosynthesis
husbandry of smaller livestock such as sheep and goats
farm where smaller livestock (like sheep, goats and sometimes pigs) are raised
a carcass, the bodily remains of an animal (sometimes including humans)
a wire frame made of metal wire covered in cloth
royal house (part of a royal family considered eligible for rule)
chainganger, somebody put to work in a chain gang (a common punishment in colonies)

kettingganger.e: Early 19th century at the latest. From ketting (“chain”) + -ganger (“-goer”).

s.def: Used other than figuratively or idiomatically: see kouwe, kak.

kouwe.e: Variant of koude with colloquial d-weakening; compare gouwe ouwe, for example.

s.def: myopic, nearsighted (also figuratively, including as an insult)
very healthy, very well, in excellent health
cart track (basic type of dirt road)
Christmas angel; figurine of an angel used as Christmas decoration (often hung in Christmas trees)
any other angel in relation to Christmas
a paper proof of payment printed by a till (cash register)

examples: Onze Niels reed half koers, met nog vijf rondjes te “knoeften”, moederziel alleen, met een halve minuut voorsprong.

2012 December/2013 January, Maarten Bernaerts, "Een tekst schrijven, dat is knoeften", Gazet van Zurenborg, page 32.
Bert Dockx: “Om de zoveel maanden komt er eens een tekst vlot uit. Maar voor de rest is dat knoeften. Dan ben je soms uren bezig zonder resultaat.”

s.def: one who pays the price (for actions or decisions made by others)
examples: Die hoge lui ontspringen de dans, maar wij zijn het kind van de rekening.

knijpbril.e: Compound of knijpen (“to pinch”) + bril (“pair of glasses”).

examples: (Citaat uit FC De Kampioenen, een VRT komische televisieserie) "Bende krabbekokers…" zei Boma. (refererend naar zijn café ploeg)

klimaatkanarie.e: From klimaat (“climate”) + kanarie (“canary”), calque of English climate canary. Referring to the canary in the coal mine.

kruipkot.e: From "kot", a shack, with the ceiling so low one couldn't stand up straight.

klein-azië.e: From klein + Azië, calque of Latin Asia Minor.

s.def: small representatives of whatever sort, fe. furniture, pottery, fish etc.
war action, action taken during a war as part of the war effort
put in a frame (as of picture etc.)
get (a shot, the ball) between the goalposts and crossbar|

s.def: to bully, to pester, to victimize

koeioneren.e: Borrowed from French coionner or couillonner (“to cheat, dupe, rip off”). Cognate to German kujonieren.

s.def: an artificial mother for infant animals, in particular livestock, to allow attachment

kenau.e: From Kenau Simonsdochter Hasselaer, after her defence of Haarlem (1572-1573).

examples:
2010, Herziene Statenvertaling, 1 Koningen 16, xiv.
Het overige nu van de geschiedenis van Ela, alles wat hij gedaan heeft, is dat niet beschreven in het boek van de kronieken van de koningen van Israël?
De kronieken van het middeleeuwse klooster beschrijven het dagelijks leven van de monniken.

kroniek.e: From Middle Dutch cronike, borrowed from Latin chronica, from Ancient Greek χρονικόν (khronikón), neuter of χρονικός (khronikós).

examples: (article title in HLN) "Sommige bestuurslui in België zijn kwistenbiebels"
(Brabantian)

church choir (choir of singers affiliated to a church)

examples: "'n Zwiert zo nie me ave kabas, moeten al a boeke kapot misschien?!"

any disliked political compromise or the act of negotiating those
beer glass resembling a volumetric flask with a round bottom and a separate wooden holder; similar to a short ell glass or yard of ale
coach driver, coacher (someone who drives a coach)

koetsier.e: Borrowed from German Kutschier, variant of Kutscher. Equivalent to koets + -ier.

examples: Da's ne kadee sè, m'n neefke!
Da's kadee!

klimaatwet.e: From the 2000s. Compound of klimaat (“climate”) + wet (“law”).

s.def: talk in a low whisper, esp. to gossip or scheme
examples: Wa zitte gulle daa na wee te konkelfoeze, ede wee nix beiters te doen?
Neukt a speelgoed weer ni kapot, gij sèn apsjaar!
Amai, was die kort van stof, 'k was bekan mijne neus kwijt—zeker wee ni gemeuge van z'n vra...

kiekeboe.e: From kieken, dialectal variant of kijken + boe.

s.def: a painter specialised in painting houses and objects (including decorative painting)
pill bug, woodlouse: any member of the Isopoda, isopod

keldermot.e: Compound of kelder (“cellar”) + mot (“sow”). Cf. English sowbug.

klassenstrijd.e: 19th century. From klasse + -n- + strijd, calque of German Klassenkampf.

kelderzeug.e: Compound of kelder (“cellar”) + zeug (“sow”). Cf. English sowbug.

kaalslag.e: Verbal noun from kaalslaan, equivalent to kaal + slag.

s.def: gambling establishment where a game similar to backgammon was played

kwaakschool.e: From Middle Dutch quaecschool, quaecscool, quaecscole, queecscool.

wrinkle at the outer corner of the eye and at the temple
a currant, a small dried grape
a miser, a scrooge, a niggard

krent.e: Borrowed from French raisin de Corinthe (literally “grapes of Corinth”).

knakworst.e: Mentioned by Kiliaen. From knakken (“to crack”) + worst (“sausage”), calque of German Knackwurst.

s.def: to partially break without fully breaking

knakken.e: From Middle Dutch knaken, probably ultimately related to Proto-West Germanic *knokōn (“to knock, beat on”).

s.def: breeding-ground, nursery (place where a certain kind of person or thing is raised or formed)
castle farm (farm leased or operated by a liege)

kopvod.e: Compound of kop (“(animal) head”) + vod (“rag”). Reportedly, it was originally used for a nun's veil in Limburg.

s.def: blowfish, puffer, any fish of the Tetraodontidae
a type of mulled wine with cinnamon (historically used to celebrate the birth of a child)
a type of mulled wine with cinnamon (historically used to celebrate the birth of a child)
administrative regional electoral district (often of considerable size)

voting application; an online survey based on the election manifestos of political parties, mostly created by politically independent foundations prior to elections, which voters can complete to find out which political party best matches their personal views

kieswijzer.e: Compound of kiezen (“to vote, to elect”) + wijzer (“indicator”).

examples: In de nasleep van de financiële crisis verhoogde die regering de kapitaaleisen aanzienlijk.

s.def: crossbower, a crossbowman or crossbow woman
elite (division of an) army or militia
burdock, plant of the genus Arctium

klit.e: From Middle Dutch clitte. Compare German Klette.

knokken.e: First attested in the early 18th century. Probably borrowed from English knock. The simplification of /kn/ to /n/ was already widespread around 1700, but not at all general. (In fact, some northern English dialects retained /kn/ until the recent past.) Moreover, the word may have been borrowed in...

s.def: A dogwood, cornel; any plant of the genus Cornus, particularly Cornus sanguinea and Cornus mas.

krabpaal.e: Compound of krabben (“to scratch”) + paal (“pole, post”).

s.def: crown jewel, one of a set of regalia
core ideal, the part(s) of a political platform that are considered essential
building where disinterred (human) bones are stored, usually at a cemetery; bonehouse, charnel house

knekel.e: From kneukel, related to knokkel and knook.

s.def: a dance and music genre evolved from kaseko, characterised by sexually suggestive lyrics and dance movements

kabula.e: Unadapted borrowing from Sranan Tongo kabula.

s.def: privateer ship, a ship authorised by a letter of marque to attack certain foreign ships
to whine like a dog or other canine species
biscuit tin (metal container for biscuits, cake, cookies, etc.)
An expletive generally used in attributive position; fucking, shit, damn
examples: Die kaolo koala's vreten m'n hele boom leeg!

koolstofmonoxide.e: Compound of koolstof + monoxide. Probably a calque.

koolstofdioxide.e: Compound of koolstof + dioxide. Probably a calque.

kooldioxide.e: Compound of kool + dioxide. Probably a calque.

s.def: the wish or desire to have one or more children
the wish or desire to have one or more grandchildren
examples: 1999: "Pa met kleinkinderwens achtervolgt dochter", in Volkskrant, 1 November 1999
2004: "Onvervulde kleinkinderwens komt steeds meer voor", in De Morgen, 5 November 2004

kleinkinderwens.e: From kleinkind (“grandchild”) + -er- + wens (“wish, desire”), formed analogously to kinderwens.

kantoortuin.e: From kantoor (“office”) + tuin (“garden”), calque of German Bürolandschaft.

kroost.e: From Middle Dutch croos, from Old French croist, from croistre, from Latin crēscere (“to grow”).

examples: Er werden koekjessnijders in de vorm van hakenkruizen verkocht en kerstballen met propaganda erop.
[…] en leeſt een Meklinker tuſſchen twe Klinkers tot den voorſten in kortſchrift: […]

kortschrift.e: From the 17th century. Compound of kort + schrift.

s.def: Initialism of Korps landelijke politiediensten, the name of the Dutch police from 1993 to 2013

kegelen.e: From Middle Dutch kēgel. Equivalent to kegel + -en.

s.def: a village in Horst aan de Maas, Limburg, Netherlands

kroaat.e: Borrowed from German Kroate. Ultimately from Proto-Slavic *xъrvatъ. Doublet of kravat.

s.def: a relationship with repeated interruptions because of falling outs and/or breaks; an on-and-off relationship

klein-zundert.e: First attested as lutelsundert in 1298. Compound of klein (“little, lesser”) and the toponym Zundert.

kaarschot.e: First attested as karlescot and karlscot in 1279. Compound of Middle Dutch Karle (a personal name) and schoot (“piece of land that juts upward”) or schot (“enclosed area, fenced-in land”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zundert, North Brabant, Netherlands

klein-oekel.e: First attested as cleen oekel in 1363. Compound of klein (“small, lesser”) and Oekel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

kalsdonk.e: First attested as caluesdonc in 1266. Compound of kalf (“calf of a cow”) and donk (“sandy hill protruding above a swamp”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

kortendijk.e: First attested as opten cortendijck in 1468. Compound of kort (“short”) and dijk (“elevated road leading through a humid area”). So named to distinguish the (then) settlement from Langendijk.

s.def: a hamlet in Halderberge, North Brabant, Netherlands

kuivezand.e: First attested as Cuvesande in 1562. Compound of dialectal kuif (“spherical, curved”) and zand (“sand”).

s.def: a city and former municipality of Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

klundert.e: First attested as die clunder in 1537. Derived from Middle Dutch clunder (“lump, clod”) with emphatic -t. Compare Fijnaart, Kladde , Klaterbuurt, Klatering, Kletterbuurt and Kleuter.

s.def: a village in Steenbergen, North Brabant, Netherlands

kruisland.e: First attested as Cruyslande in 1501. Compound of kruis (“cross”) and land (“land”). The name may have been coined as a reference to the large sums of money that the inhabitants of the settlement were forced to pay in order to ransom the owner of the village, Engelbrecht of Nassau (1451-1504).

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

kijkuit.e:
* (Noord-Brabant) Attested as De Kykuit in 1838-1857. Compound of the imperative form of kijken (“watch, look”) and uit (“out”), here used as a synonym of uitkijk (“crow's nest, look-out”).
* (Zeeland) Attested as Kijkuit in 1747. Compound of the imperative form of kijken (“watch, look”) and uit ...

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Woensdrecht, North Brabant, Netherlands

korteven.e: First attested as corttevenne around 1400. Compound of kort (“short”) and ven (“small pool in a forest or heath”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

kerkwerve.e:
First attested as kiricwereve in the first half of the 11th century. Compound of kerk (“church”) and werf (“artificial hill for habitation”).
See also Zealandic Kaerkwaerve, Kaarkwaarve.

s.def: a former village and former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

klaaskinderkerke.e: Compound of Klaas (a personal name), the plural form of kind (“child”) and kerk (“church”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

kaaskenswater.e: Attested as Kaaskens Water in 1839-1859. Compound of a diminutive form of kaas (“cheese”) and water (“water, watercourse”). Originally a hydronym.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Noord-Beveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

kortgene.e:
First attested as cortkeen and cortekene in 1247. Compound of kort (“short”) and keen (“gully, creek”). Originally a hydronym.
See also Zealandic Kortjeen.

s.def: a village in Noord-Beveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

kamperland.e: First attested as campan in 976. Compound of a toponym derived from the dative plural form of kamp (“enclosed piece of land”) and land (“land”). The first element of the toponym is the name of the former village of Kampen, which was destroyed by flooding in 1530 and 1532.

s.def: a former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

koudekerke.e:
First attested as coldekirca in 1181-1210. Compound of Old Dutch cold (“cold, in disuse, abandoned”) and kirca (“church”).
See also Zealandic Koukerke.

s.def: a hamlet in Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

klein-valkenisse.e: Attested as Klein-Valkenisse in 1838-1857. Compound of klein (“small, lesser”) and a compound toponym derived from Middle Dutch Valke (a personal name) and nisse (“headland, spit”). The element klein was added to distinguish the settlement from Groot-Valkenisse.

s.def: a hamlet in Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

krommenhoeke.e: First attested as crummenhoec in 1343. Compound of krom (“bent”) and hoek (“corner”). The settlement was named for its location close to the bend of a levee.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Middelburg, Zeeland, Netherlands

kleverskerke.e: First attested as clawarskerke in 1251. Compound of Middle Dutch Clawaert (a personal name) and kerk (“church”).

s.def: a village in Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

kwadendamme.e:
Attested as Quaendamme in 1581. Compound of Middle Dutch quaet (“in disrepair”) and dam (“dam”). Compare Vuilendam.
See also Zealandic Kwedamme, Kwîdamme.

s.def: a hamlet in Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Goes, Zeeland, Netherlands

kattendijke.e:
First attested as cattindic in 1214. Compound of kat and dijk (“levee”). The element kat is difficult to interpret; it may derive from Middle Dutch katte (“earthen mound supported by beams and poles”) or be used in a metaphorical sense to refer to the poor quality of the levee. Compare Cadzand.
S...

s.def: a village and former municipality of Goes, Zeeland, Netherlands

kloetinge.e:
First attested as clotinge in 1216. Derived from Old Dutch *klôta (“lump, round object”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-, here referring to an elevated location. Compare Kruiningen and Teteringen.
See also Zealandic Kloetehen, Kloetehe.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Reimerswaal, Zeeland, Netherlands

krabbendijke.e:
First attested as crabbendic in 1187. Derived from the engineering term krabbendijk (“sea defence consisting of wooden poles located in front of the main levee”). Compare Krabbendam.
See also Zealandic Krabbendieke, Krabb'ndieke.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Reimerswaal, Zeeland, Netherlands

kruiningen.e:
First attested as cruninc in 1204. Derived from Old Dutch crune (“crown, crest”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingô-. Compare Kloetinge and Teteringen.
See also Zealandic Krunihe, Krunige, Krunehe.

s.def: a village in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

kapellebrug.e: First attested as ter eecken in 1459. Compound of kapel (“chapel”) and brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a village in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

kloosterzande.e: First attested as terra que Sand et Grotha appelatur in 1170. Compound of klooster (“monastery”) and zand (“sand bar, alluvial sand deposit”). The element klooster refers to a possession of the Ter Duinen abbey.

s.def: a village in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

kuitaart.e: First attested as up coytaerde in 1469. Etymology unknown. Potentially derived from a hydronym.

s.def: a hamlet in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

kalverdijk.e:
* (Noord-Holland) Attested as Caluerdyck in 1575. Compound of kalf (“calf”) and dijk (“levee”). Named after a nearby levee.
* (Zeeland) Compound of kalf (“calf”) and dijk (“levee”). Named after a nearby levee.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

kamperhoek.e: Attested as Camperhoek in 1865. Compound of the toponym Kampen and hoek (“corner”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

knuitershoek.e: Attested as Knuitershoek in 1838-1857. Compound of dialectal knuiter (“common linnet, Linaria cannabina”) and hoek (“corner”). Compare Fluitershoek.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

krabbenhoek.e: Attested as KRABBENHOEK in 1845. Compound of either the plural form of krab (“crab”) or Krabben (a surname) and hoek (“corner”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

kreverhille.e: Attested as Kreverhille in 1844. Compound of either an otherwise unknown toponym *Kreve or Krever (a surname) and hil (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

kruisdorp.e: Attested as Cruijsdijcke in 1664. Compound of kruis (“cross”) and dorp (“village, settlement”). The settlement was named for its location in the Kruispolder.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

kruispolderhaven.e: Attested as Kruis Polder in 1838-1857. Compound of the toponym Kruispolder and haven (“harbour”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

koewacht.e: Attested as De Coewagt in 1640. Compound of koe (“cow”) and wacht (“watch, guard”).

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

kamperse hoek.e: Attested as Camperhoek in 1838-1857. Derived from the toponym Kampen and hoek (“corner, bend”).

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

klein gent.e: Derived from klein (“small, little”) and the toponym Gent.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

katspolder.e: Attested as de Lieven- Noord- Zuid- Serlippens- en Kats-Polder in 1740. Compound of Cats (a surname) and polder (“polder”). The polder was built by Jan Peeters van Cats in 1613.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

kapitalendam.e: Attested as Kapitalendam in 1838-1857. Compound of kapitaal (“chief, main”) and dam (“dam”).

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

klakbaan.e: Attested as Klakbaan in 1936. Compound of klak (“slush, mud spatter”) and baan (“thoroughfare”). The settlement was named for its location next to the eponymous road.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

klein-brabant.e: Attested as Klein Braband in 1838-1857. Likely a compound of klein (“small, little”) and the toponym Brabant.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

koninginnehaven.e: First attested as Koninginnehaven. Compound of koningin (“queen”) and haven (“harbour, port”). Named after Emma of Waldeck and Pyrmont (1858-1934).

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

kruisdijk.e:
Attested as Kruisdijk in 1838-1857. Compound of kruis (“cross, crossing”) and dijk (“levee”). The settlement is located next to a crossing of two levees.
See also Zealandic Kruusdiek.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Lelystad, Flevoland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

kruidenwijk.e: Compound of the plural form of kruid (“herb”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after various herbs.

s.def: a hamlet in Dronten, Flevoland, Netherlands

ketelhaven.e: Attested as Ketelhaven in 1950. Compound of the hydronym Ketelmeer and haven (“harbour, port”).

s.def: a village in Noordoostpolder, Flevoland, Netherlands

kraggenburg.e: First attested as Kraggenburg in 1950. Named after a former emergency harbour constructed in 1847, the name of which derives from a compound of dialectal krag (“floating peat, clump of reeds”) and burg (“manor”).

s.def: a hamlet in Kampen, Overijssel, Netherlands

kampereiland.e: Compound of the toponym Kampen and eiland (“island”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Kampen, Overijssel, Netherlands

kamperveen.e: First attested as in veno prope campen and camperavene in 1236. Compound of the toponym Kampen and Middle Dutch veen (“peat bog”).

s.def: a village in Zwartewaterland, Overijssel, Netherlands

kamperzeedijk-oost.e: First attested as Afsched of de Kamperzeedijk in 1843. Compound of the toponym Kampen, zeedijk (“maritime levee”) and oost (“east”). The element oost was added to distinguish the settlement from Kamperzeedijk-West.

s.def: a village in Zwartewaterland, Overijssel, Netherlands

kamperzeedijk-west.e: First attested as Afsched of de Kamperzeedijk in 1843. Compound of the toponym Kampen, zeedijk (“maritime levee”) and west (“west”). The element west was added to distinguish the settlement from Kamperzeedijk-Oost.

s.def: a hamlet in Zwartewaterland, Overijssel, Netherlands

kievitsnest.e: First attested as Kievitsnest in 1899. Compound of kievit (“northern lapwing, Vanellus vanellus”) and nest (“nest”). Originally the name of a house. Compare Kievitshaar.

s.def: a village in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

kallenkote.e: First attested as coldenkote in 1428. Compound of Middle Dutch kald (“cold, abandoned”) and the dative singular form of kot (“cottage, shack, small farm”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

kuinre.e: First attested with certainty as kuinre in 1119. Derived from a hydronym of uncertain etymology. Potentially derived from Proto-Indo-European *kewen, from whence Latin cuneus (“wedge”) and Dutch schoon (“clean, pure”).

s.def: a former municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands
a hamlet in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

* (Noord-Holland) First attested as quaecoelen in 1373. Potentially a compound...

s.def: a hamlet in Hoogeveen, Drenthe, Netherlands
a hamlet in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

kalenberg.e:
* (Drenthe) First attested as Kalenberg in 1846. Compound of kaal (“bald, barren”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”).
* (Overijssel) First attested as KALEMBERG, Kalenburg, Kalenburg of Kalenberg in 1845. Compound of kaal (“bald, barren”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). See also Dutch Low Saxo...

katerveer.e: First attested as Caetense Veer in 1570. Compound of the toponym Katen and veer (“ferry, crossing”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

kamperpoort.e: First attested as Kamperpoortenvoorstad around 1700. Compound of the toponym Kampen and poort (“gate”). Named after one of the former gates of Zwolle.

s.def: a village in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

kloosterhaar.e: First attested as De Klooster Haar in 1868. Compound of klooster (“monastery”) and haar (“sandy ridge”). The area where the current settlement is located was formerly owned by the Cistercian abbey at Sibculo.

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

keiendorp.e: Compound of the plural form of kei (“boulder, rock”) and dorp (“village, settlement”). The settlement was named for the rocky soil on which it was built.

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

kievitshaar.e: Potentially attested as kyvedeshaar in 1337, attested with certainty as Kiewits Haar in 1830-1855. Compound of kievit (“lapwing, Vanellus vanellus”) and haar (“sandy ridge”). Compare Kievitsnest.

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

kloosterdijk.e: First attested as Kloosterdijk in 1899. Compound of klooster (“monastery”) and dijk (“levee”). The area where the current settlement is located was formerly owned by the Cistercian abbey at Sibculo.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Olst-Wijhe, Overijssel, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

knutteldorp.e: Compound of Knuttel (a surname) and dorp (“village, settlement”). The neighbourhood was named after its planner, architect Willem Pieter Cornelis Knuttel (1886-1974).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

keizerslanden.e: Compound of keizer (“emperor”) and the plural form of land (“land”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

klein douwel.e: First attested as lutteke douvelde in 1399. Derived from klein (“small, lesser”) and a compound of Middle Dutch dauw (“dew”) and veld (“field”). The element klein was added to distinguish the settlement from Groot Douwel.

s.def: a hamlet in Hof van Twente, Overijssel, Netherlands

kerspel goor.e: Derived from kerspel (“parish”) and the toponym Goor.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hof van Twente, Overijssel, Netherlands

kerkelanden.e:
* (Noord-Holland) Attested as Graafelijkheids en Naarder Kerkelanden in 1750. Compound of kerk (“church”) and the plural form of land (“land”). The toponym refers to a section of land donated to the church of Naarden in 1481.
* (Overijssel) Compound of kerk (“church”) and the plural form of land ...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

kluppelshuizen.e: First attested as kluppelshuis in 1459. Compound of Middle Dutch cluppel (“club”) and the plural form of huis (“house”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

kanaalzijde.e: Compound of kanaal (“canal”) and zijde (“side”). The neighbourhood was named for its location next to the Twentekanaal.

s.def: a hamlet in Tubbergen, Overijssel, Netherlands
a hamlet in Dinkelland, Overijssel, Netherlands

klein agelo.e: First attested as luttiken agele in 1408. Derived from klein (“small, lesser”) and a compound of Old Dutch Ago (a personal name) and lo (“light forest”). The element klein was added to distinguish the settlement from Groot Agelo.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands

kotman.e: First attested as jan ten katte in 1472. Compound of kot (“cottage, shack, small farm”) and man (“man, inhabitant”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hengelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

klein driene.e: First attested as Klein Driene in 1830-1855. Derived from klein (“small, lesser”) and the toponym Driene. The element klein was added to distinguish the settlement from Groot Driene.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

krooswijk.e: First attested as Krooswijk in 1866. Compound of kroos (“duckweed, Lemna sp.”) and wijk (“secondary canal”). Compare Crooswijk.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

kievitsloop.e: Derived from the hydronym Kievitsloop, an older name of a watercourse currently called Moerenloop.

kastelenbuurt.e:
* (Limburg) Compound of the plural form of kasteel (“castle”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous castles.
* (Noord-Brabant) Compound of the plural form of kasteel (“castle”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are...

a neighbourhood of Venlo, Limburg, Netherlands

kloosterkwartier.e: Compound of klooster (“monastery”) and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”). The territory which the neighbourhood stands on formerly hosted several monasteries.

kortenoord.e:
* (Zuid-Holland) Attested as Corte Noord in 1683. Compound of an inflected form of kort (“short”) and oord (“land protruding into or bordering water”). Compare Langenoord.
* (Gelderland)

kolderveen.e: First attested as coldervene in 1362. Compound of koud (“cold”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

kolderveense bovenboer.e: First attested as Colderveense Bovenboer in 1811-1813. Derived from the toponym Kolderveen and a compound of boven (“upper”) and dialectal boer (“hamlet”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Meppel, Drenthe, Netherlands
a hamlet in Westerveld, Drenthe, Netherlands

kalteren.e: First attested as curie, que calthorne dicitur in 1209. Etymology uncertain. Potentially a compound of Proto-Germanic *kalwa (“bald, barren”) and *þurnu- (“thorn, thorn bush”). Compare Collendoorn and Kallenbroek.

s.def: a village in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands

koekange.e: First attested as Middle Dutch kukange in 1290, a borrowing from Old French Cocagne (“Cockaigne, mythical land of plenty”). The name was picked by officials in the Prince-Bishopric of Utrecht in order to encourage farmers to move to the new settlement. Compare Bonrepas, Kockengen, Kudelstaart, Sc...

s.def: a village in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands

koekangerveld.e: First attested as Koekanger Veld in 1851-1855. Compound of the toponym Koekange and veld (“field”).

s.def: a village in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands

kerkenveld.e:
First attested as Duivelsveer in 1811-1813. Compound of kerk (“church”) and veld (“field”). The area was originally called Duivelsveer, a compound of duivel (“devil”) and veer (“ferry, crossing”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Karkenveld.

s.def: a hamlet in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands
a hamlet in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands

kraloo.e:
* (near Pesse) First attested as domus de craenlo in 1298-1304. Compound of kraai (“crow, Corvus corone”) and lo (“light forest”).
* (near Ruinerwold) First attested as over crallynger marcke in den kerspele van Meppelen in 1428. Compound of kraai (“crow, Corvus corone”) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hoogeveen, Drenthe, Netherlands

krakeel.e: Attested as het Krakeelsche Opgaande in 1797. Derived from krakeel (“argument, conflict”). The toponym indicates that the land was contested between two parties. Compare Cabauw, Hebrecht, Kijfhoek, Kijfwaard, Kibbelgaarn, Kibbelveen, Krakeelpolder, Schellach and Strijkviertel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hoogeveen, Drenthe, Netherlands

kinholt.e: Attested as in 't Kinholt in 1511. Etymology uncertain. Potentially a compound of Middle Dutch kine (“gully”) and holt (“rising deciduous forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hoogeveen, Drenthe, Netherlands

kattouw.e: Attested as Jan Hendriks Kattouw in 1691. Derived from the German surname Kattau. Originally the name of an immigrant family which manned a sluice near Hoogeveen. The surname was gradually applied to the sluice, and then to the neighbourhood around the sluice.

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Drenthe, Drenthe, Netherlands

klatering.e: First attested as toe Klateringe in 1553. Derived from Middle Dutch clatering (“land which floods regularly”). Compare Kladde, Klaterbuurt, Kletterbuurt, Kleuter and Klundert.

s.def: a hamlet in Coevorden, Drenthe, Netherlands

kibbelveen.e:
Compound of kibbelen (“squable, fight”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”). The toponym refers to a marsh which was the subject of a boundary dispute. Compare Cabauw, Hebrecht, Kijfhoek, Kijfwaard, Kibbelgaarn, Krakeel, Krakeelpolder, Schellach and Strijkviertel.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Kibbelvene.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Coevorden, Drenthe, Netherlands

klinkenvlier.e: First attested as an clencke vleder in 1411. Compound of either Middle Dutch clinge (“hilly geest”) or klenke (“hilly heathland”) and vlier (“swampy peat, moor used as a pasture”).

s.def: a village in Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

klazienaveen-noord.e: Compound of the toponym Klazienaveen and noord (“north-”).

s.def: a hamlet in Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

koelveen.e: First attested as Koelveen in 1899. Compound of a dialectal variant of kuil (“pit, hole”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”). The toponym refers to a section of bog that had been excavated.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

kamerlingswijk.e: Attested as Kamerlingwijk of Hoofdwijk C in 1936. Compound of Kamerling (a surname) and wijk (“secondary canal in a turf extraction area”). Named after a nearby canal.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

kloostermanswijk.e: Attested as Kloostermanswijk of Hoofdwijk C in 1936. Compound of Kloosterman (a surname) and wijk (“secondary canal in a turf extraction area”). Named after a nearby canal.

s.def: a village in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

klijndijk.e:
First attested as Klijndijk in 1899. Compound of Klijn (a surname) and dijk (“levee”). The settlement is named after entrepreneur Jasper Klijn (1810-1858).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Klijndiek.

s.def: a hamlet in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

kavelingen.e: First attested as De Kavelingen in 1851-1855. Derived from the plural form of kaveling (“plot of land”). Originally the name of a field.

s.def: a hamlet in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

kostvlies.e: First attested as Kost Vleesch around 1832. Compound of kost (“cost”) and vlees (“meat”). The toponym refers to the difficulty of developing land around the village. Compare De Jammer and Kostverloren.

s.def: a hamlet in Noordenveld, Drenthe, Netherlands

klunderveen.e: First attested as Het Klundersveen in 1899.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Assen, Drenthe, Netherlands

kloosterveen.e: Attested as Cloosterveen in 1765-1773. Compound of klooster (“monastery”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”). Named after a nearby monastery. Formerly an independent hamlet.

s.def: road tax that incorporates some measure of travelled distance into pricing
a hamlet in Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

klein harkstede.e: Derived from klein (“small, lesser”) and the toponym Harkstede.

s.def: a hamlet in Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

kröddeburen.e: First attested as Croddebuiren around 1685. Derived from Dutch Low Saxon krödde (“weeds”) and the plural form of buur (“cottage, shack”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

korrewegwijk.e: Attested as Curren wech and Curren hoff in 1503. Compound of Curre (a personal name), weg (“way, road”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood is named after a road running through it. Compare Cornjum, Korhorn and Kornhorn.

s.def: a hamlet in Oldambt, Groningen, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Amstelveen, North Holland, Netherlands

kostverloren.e: * (Oldambt) Attested as Kostverloren in 1899. Compound of kost (“cost, price”) and verloren (“lost”). The toponym refers to a section of land which was either difficult to till or which lost its use as soon as it had been reclaimed. Compare De Jammer and Kostvlies. See also Dutch Low Saxon Kostve...

s.def: a village in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

kolham.e:
* (Midden-Groningen) First attested as in hamme in 1266. Compound of Middle Dutch cald (“cold”) and ham (“alluvial land in the bend of a river”). Compare Koehool and Kolhol.
* (Het Hogeland) First attested as Kolham in 1781. Difficult to interpret due to a paucity of old attestations. Potentially...

s.def: a village in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

kropswolde.e: First attested as crepeswalda in 1249. Compound of Old Frisian Crepo (a personal name) and wold (“marsh forest, swamp forest”).

s.def: a village in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

kiel-windeweer.e: Compound of the toponyms Kiel and Windeweer.

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

kalkwijk.e: First attested as op de Kalkwijk in 1706. Compound of kalk (“chalk”) and wijk (“secondary canal in a turf extraction area”). The hamlet is named after a nearby chalk oven.

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

kleinemeer.e: First attested as Kleine Sap meer around 1660. Compound of klein (“little, lesser”) and meer (“lake”). Originally a hydronym.

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

kibbelgaarn.e: First attested as Kibbelgaarn in 1867. Compound of Dutch Low Saxon kibbel (“squabble, fight”) and gaarn (“garden”). The settlement is located in an area which was the subject of multiple boundary disputes. Compare Cabauw, Hebrecht, Kijfhoek, Kijfwaard, Kibbelveen, Krakeel, Krakeelpolder, Schellac...

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

korengarst.e: First attested as Corengarst in 1665. Compound of koren (“corn”) and a dialectal variant of geest (“elevated sandy land”).

s.def: a hamlet in Veendam, Groningen, Netherlands

korte akkers.e: First attested as Korte akkers in 1867. Derived from kort (“short”) and the plural form of akker (“farmland”). The toponym refers to a complex of farmland.

s.def: a hamlet in Pekela, Groningen, Netherlands

kruiselwerk.e: First attested as Kruiselwerk in 1867. Likely derived from kruiswerk (“canal with many branching sub-canals”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

klein-ulsda.e: First attested as Kl. Ulsda in 1851-1855. Compound of klein (“small, lesser”) and the toponym Ulsda.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

klontjebuurt.e: Compound of the diminutive form of klont (“lump”) and buurt (“hamlet, settlement”). The settlement may have been named for its dense habitation (see klonteren).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

koudehoek.e: First attested as Koude-Hoek in 1845. Compound of koud (“cold”) and hoek (“corner, isolated place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Oldambt, Groningen, Netherlands

kromme-elleboog.e: First attested as Kromme elboog in 1899. Compound of krom (“bent”) and elleboog (“elbow”). The settlement is located next to an elbow-shaped bend in a road.

s.def: a hamlet in Oldambt, Groningen, Netherlands
a village in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

krewerd.e: First attested as crewerth and crewirth in the 13th century. Compound of Old Frisian krê (“crow, Corvus corone”) and werth (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Arwerd.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

kopaf.e: First attested as De Groenweide of Kopaf in 1867. Compound of kop (“head”) and af (“off”). The toponym may refer to a place where somebody was decapitated by accident or to a place where death sentences were executed. Compare Kijkuit, De Omval, Pasop, Rolaf and Valom.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

kolhol.e: First attested as coldehol and coldeholl in 1453. Compound of Middle Dutch kolde (“cold”) and hol (“low-lying swampy land”). Compare Koehool and Kolham.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

kantens.e: First attested as cantenze in 1326. Likely derived from Old Frisian Kanto (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix *-ingi-. According to an alternative hypothesis, the toponym derives from a pre-Germanic (potentially Celtic) source. Compare Hantum and Cantaing-sur-Escaut.

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

kleine huisjes.e: First attested as Kleine huisjes in 1899. Derived from klein (“small, little”) and the plural diminutive form of huis (“house”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

kloosterburen.e: First attested as cloosterburen hammerick in 1458. Compound of klooster (“monastery”) and the plural form of buur (“house, cottage”). Named after two former monasteries.

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Lansingerland, South Holland, Netherlands

examples: De kruisweg van Jezus is een belangrijk verhaal in de Bijbel.
Tijdens de vastentijd herdenken veel christenen de kruisweg van Jezus.

s.def: Way of the Cross (stations depicting the Passion)
examples: De kerk heeft veertien stations van de kruisweg langs de muren.
De kruisweg-stations vertellen het verhaal van Jezus' laatste uren.
Tijdens de kruisweg mediteren gelovigen over het lijden van Jezus.
De kruisweg is een diepe spirituele oefening voor veel christenen.

kruisweg.e:
* (Groningen) Attested as KRUISWEG (DE) in 1845. Derived from kruisweg (“crossroads”). The settlement was named for its location next to a crossroads. See also Dutch Low Saxon Kroesweeg.
* (Zuid-Holland) Attested as KRUISWEG (AAN-DEN-) in 1845. Derived from kruisweg (“crossroads”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

kaakhorn.e: First attested as Kaakhorn in 1899. Compound of kaak (“pillory”) and horn (“corner”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

katershorn.e: Compound of Katers (a surname) and horn (“corner”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

klein garnwerd.e: First attested as Kleyn Garwert in 1677-1678. Derived from klein (“small”) and the toponym Garnwerd.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

koningsoord.e: First attested as Koningsoord (Het) in 1899. Compound of Koning (a surname) and oord (“place”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

klein wetsinge.e:
Derived from klein (“small, lesser”) and the toponym Wetsinge. The toponym contrasts with Groot Wetsinge.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Lutke Wetsen.

s.def: a village in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

kommerzijl.e: First attested as Upslachster syl in 1634. Likely a compound of West Frisian Kommer (a surname) and zijl (“discharge sluice”).

s.def: a village in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

kornhorn.e: First attested as vp Corriger sandt in 1596. Compound of Old Frisian Curre (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and horn (“corner”). Compare Cornjum, Korhorn and Korrewegwijk.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

kenwerd.e: First attested as chinicwirde in 786-787. Compound of Old Frisian chinic (“king”) and wirde (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

keuningswijk.e: First attested as Keuningswijk in 1936. Compound of Keuning (a surname) and wijk (“secondary canal in a turf extraction area”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

korhorn.e: First attested as KORHORN (DE) in 1845. Compound of Old Frisian Curre (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and horn (“corner”). Compare Cornjum, Kornhorn and Korrewegwijk.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

krassum.e: First attested as creslinge around 820. Etymology unknown. A proposed derivation from Proto-Germanic *Kraftilo (a personal name) does not match with the oldest attestations of the toponym.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

kuzemer.e: First attested as cusemaria in 1338. Potentially derived from Middle Dutch Kuse (a personal name) and maar (“watercourse”). Unlikely to be derived from a compound of kuis (“chaste”) and the name of Saint Mary.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

kuzemerbalk.e: First attested as Jouwer of Cuzemerbalk in 1851-1855. Compound of the toponym Kuzemer and balk (“bridge”).

s.def: a village in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

kootstertille.e: First attested as tille in 1508. Calque of West Frisian Koatstertille, a compound of the toponym De Koaten and tille (“fixed bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

kortwoude.e: First attested as Cortwold in 1543. Calque of West Frisian Koartwâlde, a compound of koart (“short”) and wâld (“forest”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kollum.e: First attested as colleheim around 822-825. Borrowed from West Frisian Kollum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Kolle (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kollumerpomp.e: First attested as de Pomp in 1786. Compound of the toponym Kollum and pomp (“culvert”).

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kollumerzwaag.e: First attested as zwaech in 1444. Calque of West Frisian Kollumersweach, a compound of the toponym Kollum and Old Frisian swâg (“pasture, land where cattle is kept”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

klein medhuizen.e: First attested as Cl. Medhuysen in 1491. Calque of West Frisian Lyts Midhuzen, derived from lyts (“small, little”) and a compound of Old Frisian mid (“mid-, middle”) and the plural form of hūs (“house”). The element klein was added to distinguish the settlement from Groot Medhuizen.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kettingwier.e: First attested as Katnye wierd in 1503. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian *Kattinga (a surname) and wier (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The current Frisian form of the toponym derives from a reinterpretation of the first element.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kletterbuurt.e: Calque of West Frisian Kletterbuorren, a compound of either kletter (“aspen, Populus tremula”) or klater (“soggy mud”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”). Compare Kladde, Klaterbuurt, Klatering, Kleuter and Klundert.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kollumeroudzijl.e: Calque of West Frisian Kollumer Aldsyl, derived from the toponym Kollum and a compound of âld (“old”) and syl (“discharge sluice”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

krabburen.e: First attested as Krabburen in 1899. Calque of West Frisian Krabbuorren, a compound of krab (“crab”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Dantumadiel, Friesland, Netherlands

kuikhorne.e: First attested as Kuykhorne and Kuckhorne in 1503. Borrowed from West Frisian Kûkherne, a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian horne (“corner of a levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Tytsjerksteradiel, Friesland, Netherlands

kleinegeest.e: First attested as Lutke Geest in 1543. Calque of West Frisian De Lytse Geast, derived from lyts (“small, little”) and geast (“elevated sandy land”).

s.def: a village in Smallingerland, Friesland, Netherlands

kortehemmen.e: First attested as korteham in 1444. Calque of West Frisian Koartehimmen, a compound of Old Frisian kort (“short”) and him (“inland polder with natural drainage”) or ham (“alluvial land in the bend of a river”).

s.def: a hamlet in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

kortezwaag.e: First attested as urasuagh in 1315. Calque of West Frisian Koartsweagen, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian kort (“short”) and swâg (“pasture, place where cattle is kept”). The element kort was added to distinguish the settlement from Langezwaag.

s.def: a hamlet in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

klein groningen.e: First attested as Klein Groningen in 1820. Derived from klein (“little”) and the toponym Groningen. Founded by labourers from Groningen.

s.def: a hamlet in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

kooibos.e: Calque of West Frisian De Koaibosk.

s.def: a village in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

katlijk.e: First attested as kathaleke in 1315. Borrowed from West Frisian Ketlik, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian katte (“cat, Felis domesticus”), Katte (a personal name) or kat (“type of sluice”) and leka (“natural watercourse”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

koudenburg.e: First attested as Koudenburg and Koldenburgh in 1718. Compound of koud (“abandoned, not in use”) and burg (“fortified place, castle”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

klazinga.e: Derived from West Frisian Klazinga (a surname).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

kaatsersbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of kaatser (“Frisian handball player”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous Frisian handball players.

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

kolderwolde.e: First attested as kolderwout in 1398. Calque of West Frisian Kolderwâlde, a compound of the toponym Koudum and Old Frisian wāld (“marsh forest, swamp forest”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kubaard.e: First attested as cubawerth in the second half of the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Kûbaard, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Cuba (a personal name) and werth (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kimswerd.e: First attested as kemmerswerth in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Kimswert, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Kemme (a personal name) and werd (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kornwerderzand.e: Attested as Kornwerderzand in 1936. Compound of the toponym Cornwerd and zand (“sand”). Originally the name of a sand bar later used as a construction island.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

koudum.e: First attested as coluuidum in 855. Borrowed from West Frisian Koudum, potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian col (“charcoal”) and the dative plural form of widu (“forest, brushwood”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

koufurderrige.e: First attested as koefoerde, kwfoerde and koefoerdt in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian Kûfurderrige, derived from a compound of the hydronym Kûfurd and rige (“row of houses, embankment”). The hydronym derives from a compound of ko (“cow”) and furde (“ford”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kleiterp.e: First attested as claterp in 1452. Calque of West Frisian Klaeiterp, derived from a compound of Old Frisian klāi (“clay”) and terp (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kromwal.e: First attested as Cromwal and Krum Wal in 1511. Calque of West Frisian Kromwâl, a compound of krom (“bent”) and wâl (“embankment, levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kerkeburen.e: First attested as kerkeburen in 1583. Calque of West Frisian Tsjerkbuorren, a compound of tsjerk (“church”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kleine wiske.e: First attested as Kl. Wiske in 1664. Calque of West Frisian Lytse Wiske, derived from lyts (“little, small”) and wiske (“wet pasture, pasture which floods in winter”). The element lyts was added to distinguish the settlement from Grote Wiske.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kleine gaastmeer.e: First attested as Oostereynde in 1543. Calque of West Frisian De Lytse Gaastmar, derived from lyts (“little, small”) and the toponym Gaastmar.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

knossens.e: First attested as knoestens in 1431. Borrowed from West Frisian Knossens.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

kooihuizen.e: First attested as Kooihuizen in 1851-1855. Calque of West Frisian Koaihuzen, derived in turn from a compound of koai (“cage”) and the plural form of hûs (“house”). Named after a set of birdcages.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

koudehuizum.e: First attested as Caldehuysem and Coldahuysum in 1543. Derived from Old Frisian kald (“abandoned, not in use”) and the dative plural form of hūs (“house”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

kingmatille.e: First attested as Kingma Tille in 1718. Compound of West Frisian Kingma (a surname) and tille (“fixed brudge”). The hamlet is located close to the former Kingma family castle.

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

klooster anjum.e: First attested as aninghum in the 13th century. Calque of West Frisian Kleaster Anjum, derived from kleaster (“monastery”) and a toponym derived from Old Frisian Ane (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”). Named after a former monastery.

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

klooster-lidlum.e: First attested as lidlem in 1317. Borrowed from West Frisian Kleaster-Lidlum, derived from kleaster (“monastery”) and a compound toponym of Old Frisian Liudila (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”). Named after a former Premonstratensian abbey.

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

kie.e: First attested as grata kee and liteka kee in 1417. Borrowed from West Frisian Kie, derived in turn from Old Frisian *kîth (“family land, inheritance, place of birth”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

kiesterzijl.e: First attested as Keesterzyl in 1511. Calque of West Frisian Kiestersyl, a compound of the toponym Kie and syl (“discharge sluice”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

koehool.e: First attested as t koudehol around 1429. Borrowed from West Frisian Koehoal, derived from a compound of Old Frisian kald (“cold”) and hol (“low-lying swampland”). Compare Kolham and Kolhol.

s.def: a hamlet in Harlingen, Friesland, Netherlands

koetille.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Koetille, a compound of ko (“cow”) and tille (“fixed bridge”). Named after a bridge primarily used to ferry cows over a brook.

s.def: a hamlet in Harlingen, Friesland, Netherlands

koningsbuurt.e: First attested as Koningsbuirte in 1664. Compound of Koning (a surname) and buurt (“hamlet”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ameland, Friesland, Netherlands

kooiplaats.e:
First attested as Vogelkooi in 1861. Compound of kooi (“cage”) and plaats (“place”). Originally the name of a house located close to a duck decoy.
See also West Frisian Koaipleats.

s.def: a hamlet in Terschelling, Friesland, Netherlands

kinnum.e:
First attested as kinnem in 922. Potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian *kine (“gully, creek”) and hēm (“home, settlement”).
See also West Frisian Kinum.

s.def: a hamlet in Goeree-Overflakkee, South Holland, Netherlands

kranendijk.e: First attested as De Kranendijk in 1845. Compound of the plural form of kraan (“crane, derrick”) and dijk (“levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands

kruiningergors.e: Attested as Kruininger gors in 1899. Compound of Van Cruyningen (a noble surname) and gors (“overgrown alluvial land deposited by the sea”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Barendrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

koedood.e: First attested as Nieuwe Koedood in 1839-1859. Derived from a hydronym, potentially derived in turn from a compound of koe (“cow”) and dood (“dead”). The river developed as a result of a major flood in 1373, and the hydronym may refer to cattle killed by said flood.

s.def: a hamlet in Alblasserdam, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Krimpen aan den IJssel, South Holland, Netherlands

kortland.e:
* (Alblasserdam) First attested as cortelant in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch cort (“short”) and lant (“land”). The name derives from a section of empoldered land divided into short parcels. Compare Langeland.
* (Krimpen aan den IJssel) Compound of kort (“short”) and land (“land”). The name...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Papendrecht, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Hendrik-Ido-Ambacht, South Holland, Netherlands

kruiswiel.e: Attested as Kruis Wiel in 1584. Compound of kruis (“cross”) and wiel (“kolk lake”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zwijndrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

kleine-lindt.e: Attested as de Kleine-Lindt in 1846. Compound of klein (“small”) and a toponym potentially derived from a hydronym (derived in turn from Proto-Germanic *linþja- (“flow slowly, flow softly”) or, alternatively, from a term cognate to Old High German lentî (“landing”).

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Zwijndrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

kijfhoek.e:
First attested as ecclesia danielis in 1395-1396. Compound of Middle Dutch kijf (“argument, fight”) and hoec (“corner”).
The toponym derives from a conflict over land boundaries in a land reclamation area. Compare Cabauw, Hebrecht, Kijfwaard, Kibbelgaarn, Kibbelveen, Krakeel, Krakeelpolder, Schel...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwijndrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

kort ambacht.e: First attested as kort-ambacht in 1430. Derived from Middle Dutch cort (“short”) and ambacht (“type of fiefdom”). Named after a former fiefdom.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands

klaaswaal.e: Attested as Claes Wael in 1555. Compound of Klaas (a personal name) and a variant of wiel (“kolk lake”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands

kuipersveer.e: Attested as Kuypersveer in 1715. Compound of kuiper (“cooper”) and veer (“ferry, crossing”). The first ferryman was a cooper.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

korendijk.e: Named after the Oude Korendijk polder.

s.def: a hamlet in Dordrecht, South Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Molenlanden, South Holland, Netherlands

kooiwijk.e: Attested as KOOIWIJK of Coywijk in 1845. Compound of kooi (“duck decoy”) and wijk (“side channel, inlet”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Krimpenerwaard, South Holland, Netherlands

krimpen aan de lek.e: First attested with certainty as crenpene in 1265. Derived from a term cognate to Old Saxon krampo (“hook”), Middle Dutch krampe (“hook”) and the hydronym Lek. The toponym contrasts with Krimpen aan den IJssel.

s.def: a hamlet in Krimpenerwaard, South Holland, Netherlands

kadijk.e: First attested as cadijc in 1357. Derived from Middle Dutch cadijc (“narrow dam or levee directly next to a watercourse, embankment”).

s.def: a town and municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

krimpen aan den ijssel.e: First attested as duabus villis que crimpen nuncupantur, sitis super meruede et super yssel in 1277. Derived from a term cognate to Old Saxon krampo (“hook”), Middle Dutch krampe (“hook”) and the hydronym IJssel. The toponym contrasts with Krimpen aan de Lek.

s.def: a municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

krimpenerwaard.e: Univerbation of Krimpener (“of or pertaining to Krimpen aan den IJssel”) + waard (“riverine island”).

klein hitland.e: First attested as Hitland (Groot en Klein) in 1899. Potentially derived from klein (“small, lesser”) and either dialectal Hitland (“the Shetland Islands”) or hit (“Shetland pony”) and land (“land”). The toponym contrasts with Groot Hitland.

s.def: a hamlet in Pijnacker-Nootdorp, South Holland, Netherlands

katwijkerlaan.e: Attested as Katwycker wech in the first half of the 17th century. Compound of the toponym Katwijk (referring here to a polder) and laan (“lane”).

a hamlet in Delft, South Holland, Netherlands

klein-delfgauw.e: Compound of klein (“small, little”) and the toponym Delfgauw.

a neighbourhood of Delft, South Holland, Netherlands

krakeelpolder.e:
Attested as De Krackeel Polder in 1611. Compound of krakeel (“argument, conflict”) and polder (“polder”).
Compare Cabauw, Hebrecht, Kijfhoek, Kijfwaard, Kibbelgaarn, Kibbelveen, Krakeel, Schellach and Strijkviertel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Delft, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Rijswijk, South Holland, Netherlands

kleurenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of kleur (“colour”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after colours.

kethel.e: First attested as harega in the first half of the 11th century. Originally called Hargan, derived from the dative plural form of Old Dutch harag (“holy place”). The toponym was eventually replaced by Kethel, perhaps derived from Middle Dutch ketel (“bowl-shaped depression in the landscape”) or ke...

a neighbourhood of Maassluis, South Holland, Netherlands

kapelpolder.e: Compound of kapel (“chapel”) and polder (“polder”). Named after a polder.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

katendrecht.e: First attested as catendrecht in 1199. Compound of Middle Dutch Kate (a personal name) or the plural form of kot (“small house, cottage”) and -drecht (“ferry, watercourse”).

a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

kleiwegkwartier.e: First attested as cleyweg in 1419. Compound of the toponym Kleiweg (derived from klei (“clay”) and weg (“way, road”)) and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”). Named after a nearby road.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

kreekhuizen.e: Attested as Kreek in 1851. Compound of the toponym Kreek (derived from kreek (“creek, gully”)) and the plural form of huis (“house”). Kreek was an independent hamlet before being absorbed by Rotterdam.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

karl marxbuurt.e: Compound of the name of Karl Marx and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous philosophers.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

kleinpolder.e: Compound of klein (“small”) and polder (“polder”). Named after a nearby polder.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

kralingse veer.e: Attested as het Kralingerveer in 1752. Derived from the toponym Kralingen and veer (“ferry”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands
a village in Westland, South Holland, Netherlands

kwintsheul.e: Attested as Quintsheul in 1639. Compound of the hydronym Quints (derived from Middle Dutch Quint (a personal name)) and heul (“arched stone bridge”).

a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

katwijk aan den rijn.e: First attested as apud renum catwijch in 1224. Derived from the toponym Katwijk, aan (“on”) and the hydronym Rijn. Katwijk is a compound of the difficult to interpret element Middle Dutch kat (“cat, mud, ridge, lesser, low-quality”) and wyc (“village, settlement”). aan den Rijn was added to disti...

kaag en braassem.e: Named after the village of Kaag and the Braassemermeer lake.

koudekerk aan den rijn.e: First attested as coudenkerke in 1276. Compound of Middle Dutch calt (“cold, not in use”) and kerk (“church”) with the identifier aan den Rijn ("on the Rhine").

kerk en zanen.e: Attested as Zane Polder in 1615. Derived from kerk (“church”) and a term likely derived from the toponym Zaan. Named after two polders, which were named in turn after the mills used to drain them.

kort haarlem.e: First attested as corte haerlem in 1491. Derived from Middle Dutch cort (“short”) and the toponym Haarlem. Named after the city in Noord-Holland.

korteraar.e: First attested as neder are in 1295-1296. Compound of Middle Dutch cort (“short”) and the hydronym Aar, for which see Ter Aar. The toponym contrasts with Langeraar.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

klein davelaar.e: Named after a former plantation. The toponym contrasts with Groot Davelaar.

kanga.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Kanga. Etymology uncertain. Proposed etymologies include a derivation from the term for "poverty" in a now lost creole language or a reference to the toponym Kongo.

kwarchi.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Kwarchi, derived from kwartje (“quarter”). The land the village stands on was sold for one quarter per square meter to the Phelipa family.

s.def: the capital city of Bonaire, Netherlands

kralendijk.e: First attested around 1840. Derived from a contracted compound of koraal (“coral”) and dijk (“levee”). Named after a levee.

kudelstaart.e: First attested as sconedorpe in 1237. Potentially a compound of Middle Dutch cudele (“sack-shaped fishing net, bottom of a fish basket, basket used to catch birds”) and stert (“long narrow strip of land”). The name was considered odd in the Middle Ages and the Count of Holland unsuccessfully trie...

s.def: a former municipality of North Holland, Netherlands

a village and former municipality of Wijdemeren, North Holland, Netherlands

kortenhoef.e: Potentially attested as curtenvene in 1156, attested with certainty as curtenhoven in 1235. Compound of Middle Dutch cort (“short”) and huue (“plot of land of a certain size”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

kadijken.e: Derived from the plural form of kadijk (“narrow levee directly next to a watercourse, embankment”). The neighbourhood is named after two such levees.

kinkerbuurt.e: Compound of the name of Johannes Kinker and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”).

kolenkitbuurt.e: Compound of the toponym Kolenkit and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The toponym derives from the nickname of a church located in the neighbourhood; said nickname in turn derives from kolenkit.
